issues.label: Vol. 1 #1
issues.title: The Official Panic Newsletter
issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
Welcome to the first issue of Moontimes the only official Widespread Panic information guide. Inside you'll find tour dates for the 1995 spring tour, a merchandising catalog, and lots of other Panic oriented goodies.
We intend this to not only be a way of getting information to you, our fans, but also an open forum for suggestions, questions, criticisms, artwork, or whatever, so feel free to write us at the address on the back cover.
We also want to tell you that Moontimes is a free publication created under the auspices of the Widespread Panic office and will be sent to everyone on the mailing list (about 20,000 strong) on a per tour basis (most likely four times a year) and will contain the most timely information available. This newsletter is not meant to be in competition with The Panicle, rather it is your channel for the "straight dope" from the Widespread Panic office. If setlists and song analysis are your bag, then The Panicle is for you!
So as winter fades into spring we bid you farewell until the next tour. Hope to see you on the road.
...All of us at Brown Cat
Widespread Panic in Cyberspace
Do you "surf the net?" Are you a cyberfreak? Many folks have realized that there is a multitude of areas out there in cyberspace where one can chat with others or post bulletins or just browse in a world of information.
If you subscribe to America Online, you can visit the Rock and Road forum in the ABC Online area where you will find a bulletin board pertaining to WP or you can chat with folks from all around the country in a room mediated by Dan Skolnick (AKA Shmogger). Also, in AOL, you can visit the Grateful Dead Forum and enter the Fellow Travelers folder where you will find another bulletin board dedicated to WP. Special thanks go to Geoff Gould (AKA Sweeney99) for creating this area for us!
If you subscribe to Compuserve, you can access the Capricorn Records folder in the Recording Industry Forum where great downloads (like the acoustic Christmas version of "Papa's Home") can be found.
Commendations must be given to Ben Tanen and Lee Silverman at Brown University in Providence, RI for freeing up some space on Phishnet to create Spreadnet.
The Story Of Bartab
Many of you know Todd Nance only from the solid beats he pounds for Panic, but many Athenians also recognized him as the seemingly tireless drummer for Bartab, a collection of local musicians who pool their efforts for worthy causes.
Bartab's simple beginning occurred back in 1987 at the Uptown Lounge when Panic had some time off and Todd was called upon to arrange a d kind of All Star jam which was known as "Jambalaya." The participants grew over the next few years and included members of Panic, White Buffalo, Bloodkin, Dashboard Saviours, Allgood, Hayride, and other individuals like John Keane, David Blackmon, and Vic Chesnutt.
Folks began to pick up on the experience and by 1991 Bartab had become a money making venture which made it possible for contributions to be made to several causes such as Planned Parenthood, The Athens Area Food Bank, and most notably Jay Smith, a local musician in need of a bone marrow transplant.
As Bartab's popularity grew there was a growing expectation in the minds of the fans for Widespread Panic to play as themselves, and expectation that was realized in the winter of 1994 when the boys were forced to premier new material from Ain't Life Grand due to the unexpected absence of Vic Chesnutt (they had planned on actually performing as brute.) This led to certain expectations for the next performance which was in January of this year. The show sold out within minutes despite the fact that ticket buyers were informed that Widespread would not be performing. This led to some minor disappointment and even caused one reporter from The Red & Black to mercilessly slam the event! Nonetheless a great time was had by all involved and concert goers enjoyed over three hours of music (including the public debut of brute.) all fueled by Todd's drumming.
This raises some questions concerning the future of the event, namely was this Bartab's "last call?" Todd replies, "The only reason we do Bartab is because we happen to be on vacation and there's a worthy cause, but due to the overanticipation of seeing Panic perform, it will probably be the last Bartab." However, Todd is quick to add: "The people involved always impress me, some are poor enough to warrant their own benefits! I'm sure the next time we're on break that something will happen, it just won't be called Bartab."
Whatever happens next, you can be sure that Todd will be playing drums and that there will be a worthy cause involved. All we can say is keep your eyes open and be ready for anything!
A Note To Tapers
We realize that the Panic experience is something that many of you wish to remember long after the show is over. In the interest of making things easier for all of us here are a few ground rules...First, Panic allows the taping of shows for personal use only! Please feel free to trade, but never sell a tape! Second, audience tapes are all we allow so please come with your own mics and power supply! Our crew is too busy dealing with the hassles of putting on the show to help you locate building power. Third, some venues adhere to strict union rules and often this includes a "no audio recording" clause. In these cases we try to get the union to lighten up, but sometimes it just doesn't work. These are the cases in which you will be left to your own devices to get your gear in the building (remember the days before there was a taping section and it was fun to sneak your gear in?). Fourth, remember that there are other people who came to see the show and may not want to deal with mics ten feet in the air right in front of them so please try to be considerate when choosing a location from which to tape. Lastly, have fun! Documentation is important so let the tapes roll!
Copyright © 2001
Widespread Panic / Brown Cat

issues.label: Vol. 1 #2
issues.title: Moon Times Vol. 1 #2
issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
Here's to Summer!
Greetings from sultry summertime Athens, GA, the home of fine soul food and Widespread Panic. This is the second in a series of occasional and informative postings concerning the goings on of the boys in the band and the good folks at Brown Cat, Inc. who make it all happen.
First we must apologize to those of you who did not receive last tour's issue of Moontimes until late into the tour. We had some problems with our mailing list program and printing schedule which have since been corrected. Hopefully you will be reading this with plenty of time to make plans for the summer tour.
We would also like to say thank you to everyone who saw WP on the
Spring tour . A great time was had by all and the boys enjoyed sharing the stage with many good friends such as Jack O'Pierce, From Good Homes, God Street Wine, Soulhat, Mother Hips, Junior Kimbrough, and Little Sister to name a few.
This summer's tour is relatively short but takes the boys all through the Midwest and the Rockies. Details are located on page four under the burning letters. Also inside you will find out more about Widespread Panic in cyberspace with a story about our WWW homepage. Look on the last page for a contest with a cool prize.
Don't forget that we value your opinion here at Moontimes headquarters so please send any feedback to the address on page four. Thanks for reading and we'll see you
on the road this July.
Panic On the WWW
In the first issue o Moontimes we informed you of several different online areas pertaining to WP. This time we are going to concentrate on the WWW (World Wide Web) homepage. For those of you who are not computer friendly, the WWW brings true multimedia to the internet in the form of color images, short video loops, and audio resources. We talked to Daniel Davenport of Interweb, Inc., the Atlanta based company who created the WP homepage, about what all of this means to you. According to Daniel, "We have created a media rich website with unlimited space for video and sound imaging featuring links to the text based resources such as Spreadnet." In fact Daniel has personally been in touch with Spreadnet's "really cool" administrator, Ben Tanen, and is excited at the prospect of a global WP village. Interweb's first project was an onsite uplink from Panic's Chastain Park concert in Atlanta May 6 which featured 50 digital photos uploaded during the show along with text. After the concert some 13 video clips were uploaded onto the homepage and have since been downloaded as far away as Australia, Israel, Norway, New Zealand, and the United Kingdom. Daniel says the service is getting about 150 "hits" or downloads per day which is amazing considering the fact that some of these video images can take more than an hour to download. It seems the future is unlimited as Interweb is making plans for an MBONE broadcast of a show from the upcoming Fall tour. "It requires lots of planning, but it will be worth it," says Daniel. "Basically an MBONE broadcast uses various reflector sites around the world to allow a real time audio and video stream to be accessed from the Internet so that you could watch and hear the show on your computer from anywhere as it happens." Expect much more out of the WP homepage such as interviews with band members, photographs, video loops, sound bytes, and a subscriber interaction. The service is free (depending on which online service you use to access the Internet) and is available at the following location: http://iweb.www.com/wsp. Check it out!
Copyright © 2001
Widespread Panic / Brown Cat

issues.label: Vol. 1 #3
issues.title: Moon Times Vol. 1 #3
issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
Touring, CD Releases Make For A Busy Fall!
As the dog days of summer fade into the crispy colors of fall we find the boys in the band preparing to take another jaunt into the wilds of the road amidst a flurry of Panic related cd releases. With seemingly not time off, Widespread Panic will be appearing for eight weeks up and down the east coast with many friends such as Joan Osbourne, War, and Col. Bruce Hampton & The Fiji Mariners. The tour will end with two shows for Halloween at the newly constructed Classic Center in Athens, GA October 30 and 31. Widespread Panic will be offering a limited number of tickets for both nights that will be available through our merchandise phone number. Look inside for this and all other tour related information.
During the month off, the boys in the band found time to examine live performance multitrack tapes from the last three tours. The hope is that a live album will soon be released. According to
Dave Schools : "We have different ideas about it, but I think we are all in agreement that a live disc would be the right thing to release as soon as possible." As soon as this becomes more than a tentative idea the Moontimes will let you know.
While we wait for the next official Panic disc there are a few releases featuring the boys of which you should be aware. The first is brute.'s Nine High A Pallet due out on Capricorn September, 12. More information can be found about this collaborative effort between most of the members of Widespread Panic and Athens songwriter Vic Chesnutt on the next page.
Also on the Capricorn label, HEMPILATION is a benefit/compilation cd produced with assistance from NORML (the National Organization for the Reform of Marijuana Laws) and High Times Magazine. Look for this first-ever benefit recording in support of NORML to hit the streets September, 26. The cd contains many bands performing cover versions of "hemp" classics like Bob Dylan's Rainy Day Women #12 & 35 (performed by The Black Crowes) and Sly & The Family Stone's I Want To Take You Higher (performed by Blues Traveler). Widespread Panic's donation to the disc is a rousing version of Van Morrison's And It Stoned Me , which was recorded live at soundcheck at the Vic Theater in Chicago August 4 and engineered by Timothy Powell of Metro Mobile Recording.
President of Capricorn Records Phil Walden states: "This project is not an endorsement of the usage of marijuana or any other illegal substance, but rather the belief that the criminal prosecution of marijuana imposes unjust and unfair penalties. HEMPILATION is a testament to these bands' support of NORML's efforts to reform these laws." A formal NORML board member, Walden hopes "that this project will not only be a great source of entertainment, but will also help spread the word about the many positive actions of NORML and the benefits of hemp as more than a drug."
On a sadder note, the Widespread Panic family would like to offer their condolences to the fans and family members of The Grateful Dead on the passing of founding guitarist and songwriter Jerry Garcia August 9, 1995; "further."
A Brief History Of brute.
On September 12 Capricorn Records will release the long awaited collaboration between most members of Widespread Panic and Athens songwriter Vic Chesnutt. Titled Nine High A Pallet, the collection of musicians chose to call itself brute. rather than Vic Chesnutt and Widespread Panic because, according to Chesnutt "the whole session seemed like more of a band effort..." As for the name, Schools describes it's inception as such: "We were all being real careful not to step on Vic's songs and he was being very tentative about the arrangements and we really couldn't seem to get the ball rolling so I told him to 'play like a brute!' and the rest is history."
The friendship between Chesnutt and the boys goes back a few years to 1992 when Widespread Panic checked in to Scott Stuckey's home studio in Athens to record the fist version of Papa's Home for the Flagpole Christmas cd. Stuckey gave Todd Nance a copy of Chesnutt's Drunk cd which was practically worn out on the tour bus that year. At Stuckey's urging the musicians met and discussed recording some of Chesnutt's songs for the sake of a good time. After a rapid rehearsal studio time was booked at Keane Recording where 9 Chesnutt originals and one cover song (Hoyt Axton's classic Snowblind Friend ) were recorded in only two days. "We considered doing a couple of different cover tunes but Snowblind Friend was the only song where both Chesnutt and John (Bell) knew all the words," says Schools. David Lowery and John Hickman of Cracker make a guest appearance on the song Cataclysm and Athens producer/engineer/musician supreme John Keane plays pedal steel guitar on Westport Ferry . The song Let's Get Down To Business has become a staple in the Panic set list as anyone who has seen a show in the last year knows.
A video has been produced for the album's lead track Good Morning Mr. Hard On and plans for live performances are being discussed. Until then we will have to e content with tapes of Bartab's legendary performance at the Georgia Theater this January. This is the only performance of brute. so far.
Chesnutt has recently signed to Capitol Records but has four other discs available from the independent Texas Hotel Records: Little , West Of Rome , Drunk , and his latest, Is The Actor Happy?
As for brute., "The whole ting was so much fun that we never bothered to think that the sessions might show up on a record," says Schools, "But I'm glad they did."
Copyright © 2001
Widespread Panic / Brown Cat

issues.label: Vol. 1 #4
issues.title: Moon Times Vol. 1 #4
issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
Peace To One And All!
Season's greetings from all of us here at
Brown Cat, Inc. to all of you. Thanks for a great year and a great Fall tour. Pictured above are almost all the folks who work to put together the show (our bus and truck drivers were sleeping) from management and road crew to the boys in the band. The photo was taken just before the doors opened Halloween night at the brand new Classic Center in the band's hometown of Athens, GA. For a related story see page four.
Inside this final issues of Moontimes for 1995 you will find information on the three show New Year's Eve run, the "Sit-N-Ski' tour of the Rockies which takes place in late January and early February, as well as a new trivia contest with a rare prize.
During the Fall tour Widespread Panic was truly gifted to have shared the stage with friends such as The Fiji Mariners, War, BonePony, Joan Osbourne, and Little Sister to mention only a few. During their time off the band will most likely be relaxing, preparing for the January Rockies stint, and culling even more live performances from the Fall tour. Just after the tour Mike, John Bell, Todd, and Dave got together with Vic Chesnutt and made a video to accompany the brute. track "Westport Ferry." The shoot was at the Winfrey Funeral Home in Athens and featured the four boys jamming in matching tuxedos.
So, until next time, have a great holiday and we hope to see you in Macon December 31!
Sit & Ski Tour 1996
The rumors have been flying about an acoustic tour of The Rocky Mountains...let Moontimes clarify that for you. Yes, Widespread Panic has scheduled the dates for the "Sit & Skit Tour 1996."
According to Mike Houser, "We are calling it 'Sit & Ski' because we are going to sit down onstage and do some skiing...I'm even going to try skiing!"
What Mike refers to as "sitting" is the version of Widespread Panic that most have not seen but many have heard on the radio: mostly acoustic guitars (Mike and Dave will sometimes play electric, only quiet), piano, and (often found) percussion. The band usually is seated and since it is not truly unplugged the band is loath to refer to it as "acoustic."
"We wanted to do something different and more intimate," said Houser. "We love Colorado...the fans are great and the scenery is some of the best." Fans can expect lots of new songs and more in the area of vocals. "Jojo is even threatening to pull out a different Longhair or Ragtime piano tune every night," laughed Dave Schools, "Anything could happen...mostly we wanted to strip down the electric approach and get a little closer to our friends in The Rockies because we barely see them anymore."
In addition to the intimacy of the approach the setup is reminiscent of the earliest days of Widespread Panic: John Bell on acoustic guitar, Mike on acoustic or electric, Dave on bass, and all kinds of folks beating various percussion devices. Lucky for everyone that Todd and Sunny joined the band.
Mike is quick to point out that the whole idea for the tour came about when "We were doing acoustic performances on the radio and realized that we really enjoyed the setting and were wishing we could do it on stage."
Now Panic fans will have a chance to see their band do just that and maybe even bump into one or tow of them on the ski slope!
A Spooky Halloween
It's no secret that Halloween is Widespread Panic's favorite holiday and this year's sold out two night stand at the new Classic Center in Athens was no exception. Costumes and all, the boys got down to business as the photo to the left will attest. Joined onstage by friends such as Danny Hutchens and Eric Carter (of Bloodkin), John Keane (pedal steel guitar), and David Blackmon (fiddle), four sets of hometown fun was had by all.
The celebratory atmosphere was bolstered by the fact that these were the inaugural shows of the Classic Center as well as the final shows of the Fall tour. A sidenote: One of the buildings torn down to make room for the civic center was the Mad Hatter Ballroom where Widespread Panic had their first gig with their new drummer, Todd Nance, nearly ten years ago. The gig was a benefit for an organization called Africare and the bill was shared by Widespread Panic, The Other Soon, and The Strawberry Flats (of whom John Keane was a member). It's a small world indeed! More on the impending 10 year anniversary of Widespread Panic in the next issue of Moontimes.
Some of you may know that the chicken is the root of all funk, well
John Bell is certainly a funky chicken on Halloween night. The photo at the right was furnished by Wingate Downs of the Athens Banner Herald and is reproduced here on behalf of chicken lovers everywhere! All we want to know is "Where's the tree???"
Copyright © 2001
Widespread Panic / Brown Cat

issues.label: Vol. 2 #1
issues.title: Moon Times Vol. 2 #1
issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
1996=Widespread Celebration!!
We here at MOONTIMES don't intend to sound self congratulatory, but we are giving ourselves a pat on the back. Why? Because we are a year old and, in addition, Widespread Panic is celebrating their tenth year together. As hard as it is to believe it was ten years ago that Todd Nance played his first public gig with WP (at the Mad Hatter Ballroom in Athens Feb. 9, 1986 to be exact) thus completing the core of the band which has remained solid to this day. This enabled them to begin non-stop touring which eventually gave them the title of "Hardest Working Band In The South." One could call the Feb. 9, 1996 show at the Ogden Theater in Denver during the Sit-N-Ski Tour the actual anniversary but the boys would prefer to continue the celebration throughout the year rather than make a fuss over one show. So with that fact firmly in check we invite you to help WP make the Spring and Summer tours the greatest ever.
As we are one year old there are a few people we have to thank for their invaluable time and effort: Mary Armstrong (legwork), Taylor Little at Resolution (typesetting and graphics), Wingate Downs (merchandising photography), and Dick Bernard (MOONTIMES masthead), not to mention countless photographers whose work has been and will continue to be seen in these pages. Most of all we would like to thank you, the reader, for all the comments you have sent in. Please keep them coming!
We have lots in store for you in this issue of MOON TIMES: memories of Sit-N-Ski, Spring tour dates, a contest winner, and a farewell to Bill "Gomer" Jordan, WP's monitor man and "Fine Swine" of ten years. So read on, learn, and we hope to see you this spring!
Sit-N-Ski Tour Report...No Injuries!!
A great time was had by all as WP took their sounds to the Rocky Mountains of Colorado in late January and early February of this year. Laughingly dubbed the Sit-N-Ski Tour the boys sat down and performed their material in small venues. What began as an "acoustic" tour soon became a free for all as band members performed favorite songs on any instrument they chose. Fiddler supreme Dave Blackmon joined the band in several cities giving Colorado fans their first opportunity to witness and hear the bluegrass sounds of this master. The best part of the whole tour was the fact that no one was hurt during the course of skiing, snowboarding, and snowmobiling throughout the Rockies. We feel that a picture is worth more than mere words so here are some comments from the boys.
John Bell : Constantly striving for clarity and communion in both playing and skiing.
John Hermann: It felt good to finally play my favorite and uplifting Beatles tune (Yer Blues).
Mike Houser : No one will ever forget Garton's!
Domingo Ortiz: I'm from Texas so I didn't ski...I watched Perry Mason.
Todd Nance : My butt still hurts!
Dave Schools : (To the crowd at Garton's) Anybody have any dramamine? I'm getting seasick up here!
Gomer Retires From The Road
The last show of the Sit-N-Ski tour in Crested Butte, CO marked more than just Valentine's Day - it began the retirement of
Bill "Gomer" Jordan and the end of an era in WP history. Gomer was formerly the front of the house engineer for WP, and more recently the monitor engineer. Gomer has been an invaluable member of the band for nearly ten years. The man whose laughter graces the end of "Gomero Blanco" was more than just a "sound guy" to the boys in the band as John Bell attests, "Gomer was the very first person that always put more into WP than he got out of it." Known also as "Remog" and "The Peerless Porker" (due to his love of bar-b-que), Gomer was a dictionary of sports trivia as anyone who witnessed his discussions of forgotten minor league baseball players of the sixties with Bruce Hampton could attest and a master of the subtle comeback. Mike Houser related the story of a concertgoer who asked Gomer for a cigarette and then a light. After handing the lighter to the person Gomer remarked, "Looks like all you've got is a habit."
Gomer began his career in sound reinforcement in 1973 when he worked for The Riverbottom Band. Along the way he worked with T. Graham Brown, Randall Bramblett, and ran monitors for REM when they played the I&I; Club where he was the house sound man. In 1986 he found himself working at the Uptown Lounge where Widespread Panic had begun playing Monday nights. "I remember coming into work one day and seeing JB trying to hook up a tape deck to the mixing board." Gomer says, "WP had gone out of town without a soundman and realized they needed one." His first impression of the band was wondering, "Who's dating the girl singer and how do you mike a homemade hammer dulcimer?"
Despite these problems Gomer traveled in the school bus with the boys begging, borrowing and gerry-rigging PA systems throughout the South. He remembers rebuilding the ever-decaying house system at The Post Office in Hilton Head, SC each time the band returned, often working through dinner until showtime to get it fixed. He was at every party out at David Carroll's farm in Monroe, GA and every five set gig at Jackson's Station, pushing faders until the sun came up. But, after nearly 25 years on the road this seasoned vet is ready to get off the bus. In his own words, "I never knew (WP) was going to get this popular make this a real job...I got into this business because I didn't want a job! This is the first time I've ever looked forward to getting home more than going on the road." The road will miss Gomer as will the boys in the band but WP will still reap his benefits as he will be working at the home office in Athens. Look forward to hearing his voice when ordering merchandise by phone and stay tuned for an "Ask Gomer" column in upcoming issues of MOONTIMES. In reference to his future replacement monitor man, Gomer has these words of advice, "Never cut cards with me and don't tell Sunny any Mexican jokes!"
Copyright © 2001
Widespread Panic / Brown Cat

issues.label: Vol. 2 #3
issues.title: Moon Times Vol. 2 #3
issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
Band Escapes Heat, Olympics In Studio
Welcome to the Summer 1996 edition of MOONTIMES. You may notice a few changes in this issue: first, we have gone full color, second, there are no tour dates. This is because MOONTIMES goes to press the band is preparing to enter John Keane Recording, Ltd. to work on their as of yet un-titled fifth recording. John Keane will produce the cd and it will be mixed by Cliff Norell and Keane, the same team that brought you AIN'T LIFE GRAND. There are no tour plans yet, but when the boys hit the road this October we will be the first to let you know when and where!
We cannot tell you what tunes will be included on this effort and there are too many possibilities to even guess at this point. We do know that the possibilities are endless with upwards of fifteen new originals and cover tunes making their live debut in the last few tours. Heck, the boys can't even decide what to call some of these tunes. Will it be "Tall Boy" or "Naked In The Mud?" Place your bets folks, we don't have a clue! All of these questions will be decided when the new disc hits the streets in early 1997.
What we do know is that Widespread Panic will be "gripping the nation" with the release of the new cd (for those of you who have seen Independence Day). Capricorn Records has recently linked a joint venture with Mercury Records for manufacturing and distribution of recordings so look for the new disc wherever you like to purchase music. In fact, the back catalog has already felt the effect of the new system...you will find all four albums in even the darkest of corners.
By the time you get this issue of MOONTIMES the Olympics will be over but as we go to press things are just getting warmed up here in Athens and in Atlanta as the athletes and scrutiny of the world descend upon the state of Georgia. We would like to wish all of the competitors luck as they go for the gold and hope they enjoy our southern hospitality and sweltering heat!
All of us at Brown Cat would like to thank everyone who came to the
shows this summer and helped make
tour the most fun and successful cross country adventure so far. Look for a Fall tour starting in mid-October and have a great summer!
Copyright © 2001

The Aragon will be the location of this years Panic Halloween Extravaganza. This has become one of the band's favorite nights to play, expect the unexpected. issues.text:
A Holiday Note From The Boys In The Band
Greetings from the Fall tour 1996. I am taking the rare opportunity afforded by a day off in
between shows in Knoxville, TN to write this cover text for the document you are now reading.
It's been a long and fruitful summer for us what with hosting the Olympics and recording anew
album which will hit the shelves early February of 1997.
We spent ten weeks at home in Athens recording under the watchful eye of our longtime
mentor and producer John Keane( who will also contribute to the next issue of MOON TIMES )
and then another two weeks mixing in Nashville with John and Malibu surf engineer extraordinaire
Clif Norrell assisted by Chad Brown at 17 Grand Studio on Music Row. We are Confident that
you will enjoy the sonic stew these exacting chefs have prepared for your hungry ears. I won't spoil
the surprise by telling exac tly what we have recorded, only that we think it's our best work yet and
we don't have a title for it. Work continues, as we collate live material from Spring and Summer
1996 tour with the efforts of ex- Sugar bassist and Athens producer / engineer David Barbe and
diligent assistance from Billy Field. This is a "pipe dream in progress" as we like to call it. Perhaps
someday we will put together the ultimate live album, but for now it is simply a matter of
documentation.
It's been nearly two years since the inception of MOON TIMES and in the tradition of
growth more and more responsibility is being taken by Mary Armstrong and Taylor Little for it's
publication and pleasing style. Mary is interested in your suggestions and would love to hear from
you. Inside you will find a selection of photos from the Aragon Halloween show in Chicago
courtesy of Jeremy Stein and Thomas Smith and a crossword puzzle designed by John Hermann
with you in mind. Our longtime monitor engineer Bill "Gomer" Jordan will be contributing a regular
feature called "The Bottom Line" which should afford you a more intimate view of life on the road
with Widespread Panic not to mention tidbits of sports wisdom, barbecue know-how, and visions
of redneck philosophy bordering on the fantastic. He looks forward to hearing from you and will
be glad to answer any questions of any nature in his column in future issues. Enjoy these new
changes and feel free to contribute art or thoughts.
December 29-30-31 will be a three night party culminating New Year's Eve at Atlanta's
Fabulous Fox Theater. We are pleased to proclaim the new mail order system for these show a
success. We have received far more orders than we expected and this will spur on the possibility of
a mail order for future concerts. Please remember to bear with us as we attempt to perfect this
system (this means you, tapers!). Spring tour will most likely begin in mid-March 1997 and
continue until we drop from fatigue. Details will be available in early 1997 and MOON TIMES
readers will be the first to know. Until then,
have a great holiday season and we'll see you at the
Fox!
-- Dave
The Bottom Line
Welcome to the first installment of the
The Bottom Line , a feature designed to clue you in on
the inner workings of Widespread Panic. Who better to represent these behind the scenes
remembrances than Bill "Gomer" Jordan, longtime monitor engineer and full time specialist. In future
issues. Gomer would like to respond to any questions from any walk of life with his own unique
brand of redneck philosophy.
As an editor, I feel it is necessary to preface this story by adding that when Gomer says "We
left town..." he is referring not to Athens, but to Richmond, VA. Let me explain: the whole thing
started in Macon, GA where we had just played our usual Friday night gig at the Rookery, packed
up the gear and played Saturday afternoon in the parking lot of some bar I can't remember (I swear
the owner said he we could put one of our PA cabinets on the hood of his car!), gone back to The
Rookery to play Saturday night, driven all night to Hampton, VA where most of us were shut out of
three nights of the Dead, and finally wound up scattered around my mother's house in Richmond,
VA. This is the town which he refers.
As we left town that day I had no reason to suspect that we had just embarked on "The Trip
From Hell." It was just five guys in an old Bluebird school bus with our stage equipment, a small
PA system, some bean bag chairs and a cooler leaving town on a routine trip to play Charlotte, NC;
Richmond, VA; and then, finally. Charlottesville, VA.
I had never been a superstitious person so when that black cat ran across I-85 in front of the
bus I was not as concerned about our luck as some of the other boys were. I'll admit it: I was
wrong. After the cat crossed our path, we stopped exit 223, Manson, NC for gas Bad move. After
filling up we decided to move the bus out from in front of the pump and, in the process, scraped a
little paint from the steel beam that protects the pump and tore the already worn out hose a little (the
damage to the bus required a sledgehammer to repair). All hell breaks loose and before we know it
the cops are there and the store manager is screaming,'Them dog days is over now!" at us. I guess it
could have been a racial thing, but I was never sure what the hell she meant. Anyway, it took $80 to
shut her up and get us back on the road. We should have taken the hint and gone home. Instead we
made it to the gig in Charlotte.
We arrived early the next day at the nameless nightclub and proceeded to set up our stage
gear even though there was no power yet. "No problem, I'll connect it" says the club owner. 220
volts later we start replacing fuses in our amplifiers and J B's can't be fixed. He eventually performs
the show on his acoustic into a vocal microphone. The rest of the boys had borrowed amps from
some local band. It turns out that Molly Hatchet had played the night before and the owner hooked
us up with 220 volts instead of 110. After an early morning stop at a music store for repairs, we
headed back up I-85 and I say to myself, " Tomorrow has got to be better than this." Famous last
words is what they call this.
We arrived at New Horizons in Richmond and were greeted by what I then called "theworst load-in on the East Coast." At the time I had never been to the West Coast. Basically the
load-in was up a straight and narrow stairway with about 70 uneven steps and no landing. The heat
and stale smell of beer reminded us that we were in Hell. The only thing worse than the load-in was
load out: it was pouring rain. We loaded up the bus and I thought the worst was over. I sure was
wrong a lot on this trip. The bus wouldn't start or even turn over. We would have to leave it there
overnight and since it was a crummy part of town we had to, yep you guessed it, load all the gear
back up the stairs into the club. The next morning we got the bus fixed, loaded the gear out again
and headed for Charlottesville. We stopped for gas and oil and noticed that the bus took even more
oil than usual. It turned out that the oil gauge wasn't reading anymore. As you have probably figured
out we had put in way too much oil and a few miles later we were on the side of the road. The
excess oil had blown all the seals and gaskets and we were hunting for a rental truck agency. When
JB returned with the U-Haul we loaded the gear quickly and headed, very late now, for
Charlottesville. Upon arrival we started loading in and managed to set up in time to start on time. I
noticed Mike's mom and a couple of guys with video cameras were in the crowd. It turns out she
had decided to surprise us by having the show professionally videotaped. Why did it have to be a
show when we were already late and tired anyway? Go figure, we managed to pull it off smiling,
load out successfully, and find a decent hotel to get some well deserved sleep. The"Trip From Hell"
was over. I thought.
The next day we had to get up early and trade the gear from the U-haul to a Ryder (who
was the only agency who let us get away with a one-way rental to Athens). So, with Todd at the
wheel, JB shotgun, and the rest of us riding (illegally) with the gear in the back of the truck, I once
again went to sleep thinking I would wake up in Athens. I should have known better, because at the
South Carolina/Georgia border we were awakened when the back door rolled up to the sound of
Todd and JB screaming, "Get up! The truck's on fire!!" I thought they were kidding but they
weren't. The exhaust system had gotten so hot that floorboard under the driver's feet had caught fire.
We poured the last of our beer and bottled water on it and put it out. We wondered if it was safe to
continue driving, but Athens was only 30 miles away, and facing the option of loading the gear into
yet another truck we made the decision to crank her up and head home. We reluctantly crawled
into the back again. This time nobody went to sleep.
I have never been so happy to get back to Athens in my life. I have been on other tough trips
before and since, but nothing to compare to "The Trip From Hell."
Copyright © 2001
Widespread Panic / Brown Cat

issues.text:
What's Up With This Summer?
Howdy folks....it sure is hot here in Athens despite the fact that summer does not officially
begin until June 21. June 21 is a special day in more ways than one this year....It marks the start of
Widespread Panic's summer tour in Kansas City and also the release of the video for Aunt Avis.
To find out more about these events just read on!
As far as this spring's recently
completed tour goes we have nothing but good things to
report. From the first shows in Vail, CO., to the final show in St. Paul, MN., we were greeted with
enthusiastic audiences and friendly folks. Some highlights included the record breaking night at Oak
Mountain Amphitheater in Birmingham AL., a brief but albeit spunky appearance at the highly
regarded New Orleans Jazz and Heritage Festival, a completely over-the-top crowd at The
Aragon in Chicago, and the two sold-out nights at San Francisco's infamous Warfield Theater (the
second of which was filmed for a future purpose). We would like to thank you, our friends and
fans, for making this most recent tour something special. In addition we would also like to thank
the folks from Sister Seven (formerly Little Sister), Cowboy Mouth, and From Good Homes (may
their van rest in peace) for lending their particular energies to several of the shows. That Fred
LeBlanc from Cowboy Mouth is something else, yes? A very special thank you is warranted for
our friend Dave "Damn!" Blackmon who played his fiddle for the cities of Chicago and St. Paul
thus making the end of the tour something that will not soon be forgotten.
Speaking of filming, many ticket holders in Cincinnati were slightly incensed by our
postponement of the show in order to make a video for the song Aunt Avis, the third single from
the Bombs & Butterflies cd. For this we apologize but we promise to make that show up in a big
way! To find out more about this video please look inside.
One last thing concerning our situation with the 1997 HORDE tour: it just didn't work out in
a way that we felt was fair to us as a band and to you as fans. We are greatly disappointed by the
fact that we won't get to see 30 Neil Young shows in a row, but we felt that it was more important
to give our fans their full recommended dose of Panic. So look forward to lengthy sets this summer
and cool nights under the stars. Full tour information is located inside. We appreciate all the kind
messages we have gotten regarding our decision to spend the summer playing all night most every
night. Remember that your energy is a vital part of what we do and we are always interested in
what you have to say. Don't be shy and feel free to write to us at any of the addresses provided on
the back page. Rock on!
Aunt Avis Video Due June 21
Panic fans will get a real treat come June 21 when Aunt Avis, the third single from Bombs
& Butterflies will be released. The song, written by Vic Chesnutt, will be accompanied by a color
video to be serviced to MTV and VH-1. The video was directed by longtime
Panic friend Billy
Bob Thornton who recently took home the Oscar for Best Adapted Screenplay for the film
Slingblade which he also directed and stars. Those of you who have seen Billy Bob's films or his
directorial debut in the long-form Widespread Panic Live From The Georgia Theater video know
that this man operates on his own terms. For those of you who are worried about MTV sellouts
and clichéd ideas have no fear for Billy Bob's work is an always refreshing look at the hidden
eccentricities of real people and life and this video is no surprise.
Shot in two days in Billy Bob's hometown of Benton, Arkansas, the video features the boys
as well as Vic Chesnutt and the surprise appearance of actress Laura Dern. You may know of her
work in films such as Blue Velvet, Wild At Heart, and Jurassic Park, not to mention her role in the
recently televised national "coming out" of Ellen DeGeneres. Billy Bob himself makes a cameo in
the video as well to round out the southern Gothic theme.
In addition to shooting for hours on end the boys and Billy Bob had time for a jam session
with Billy Bob's old bandmates, Mike and Nick Shipp at the end of each night. Featured were
lengthy and loose renditions of "Feelin' Alright" and "Honky Tonk Woman." We never knew Billy
Bob could play drums as well as make films! Having seen only a rough cut of the video at press
time the overall opinion is one of "Billy Bob has done it again!" While Michael Houser was slightly
injured during the
making of the video , Domingo Ortiz had this to say: "I liked it because we didn't
have to wear any make up!" Stay tuned to your favorite video music channel (yeah, right!) and
keep your eyes peeled for 'Aunt Avis".
That Time of Year
Welcome to the THIRD installment of the
The Bottom Line . A feature designed to clue you in
on the inner workings of Widespread Panic. Who better to represent these behind the scenes
remembrances than Bill "Gomer" Jordan, longtime monitor engineer and full time specialist. In future
issues. Gomer would like to respond to any questions from any walk of life with his own unique
brand of redneck philosophy.
Most of our band and crew are baseball fans of one allegiance or another. I know of
Indians, Rockies, Yankees, Cubs, and, of course, Braves fans amongst our group. We even have a
lone Mets fan. In fact I think all of those airplanes taking off and landing over Shea Stadium had
some kind of effect on Jojo who bet both Dave and I fifty bucks straight up that the Mets would win
over half of their games. This is not the wager of a rational man. Anyway it is once again that time of
year when the crack of the bat can be heard all over the states (and Japan).
Today is Tuesday, April 1, the opening day of the 1997 baseball season and I am writing this
column while watching the first televised game of the year on ESPN. The Chicago White Sox are
playing the Toronto Blue Jays at the Skydome. I am not usually a fan of "indoor" baseball but with
the weather this day in Toronto I guess I can understand the need for a dome. The TV announcers
say there is a foot of snow outside and that the entire three game series would have been canceled
had it been played outdoors. However, here in Athens, GA. it is a sunny 70 degrees, but I don't
want to brag. The weather is about the same an hour's drive southwest of here where the Atlanta
Braves have finally moved into their new "outdoor" baseball only ballpark. The only reason The
Braves would need a dome would be for air conditioning. The new park is called Turner Field,
named after you-know-who. When people at the first exhibition game compared Turner Field to
Camden Yards in Baltimore and Coors Field in Denver it made me smile because I had gone to a
game at Camden Yards and loved it. I also knew people who had been to Coors Field and had
similar feelings. I have high expectations for the new park.
Speaking of expectations I never expected to see baseball played on a field that looks like a
putt-putt course. In the game I'm now watching, they have the grounds crew out trying to pull the
carpet tighter over the playing area in right field. Players on both teams were complaining of bumps
and ridges in the artificial turf. The grounds crew had actually unzipped the field over in foul ball
territory and had eight guys trying to stretch it tighter by pulling on the edge of the carpet. Like that's
really going to work! Supposedly this rug had just been replaced and whoever put the thing back
down had obviously never "grown any grass,"....er, I mean never "laid any carpet."
I guess by now you have figured out I don't like artificial turf. I don't know anyone who
does...especially the players. That stuff is as hard as concrete. Just ask anyone who saw
Washington Redskins Quarterback Joe Theisman's leg snap when his cleats didn't give on the carpet
what they think. I know what Joe thinks: that injury ended his career.
Another thing I don't like about artificial turf is that the stuff is plain ugly. Nothing looks worse
on TV or in person than a stained carpet and I've never seen one that didn't have a few unsightly
stains. I'll never forget my disappointment when Todd, Mike, and I got to our seats at Kaufman
Stadium in Kansas City: The Royals played outdoors and they had artificial turf anyway. This didn't
make any sense to me since it was an otherwise beautiful park. The carpet looked terrible and it
was the same at Busch Stadium in St. Louis where the Cardinals play ball. Thankfully, and maybe
coincidentally both fields switched back to real grass after the player's strike. Hey....maybe that
strike accomplished something after all!
Well, the Toronto grounds crew just zipped the field back up so I guess it's about time to
"play ball." Watching this carpet stretching has really made me appreciate the fact that the team I
support gets to play on grass most of the time. Call me old-fashioned, but I guess I'm just a big fan
of natural grass.
Copyright © 2001
Widespread Panic / Brown Cat

issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
Dirk Speaks
Hope everyone enjoyed
the summer . Here at Brown Cat we are busy preparing for the fall tour and I have been granted some space in Moon Times to discuss a few thoughts. I know most of you out there have been to numerous shows and are already familiar with how things work. For the curious, my job as road manager is to organize and coordinate all the different aspects and people involved in doing a live concert. I think you will agree, there is nothing like a Panic concert and we all work hard to keep it going and growing. Often the venues we are performing at are not familiar with the music or the scene. I have worked hard to communicate a clear and positive picture of the band and their music and I must say, it seems to be happening.
As early as 7:00 am, the Panic crew rises and tries to function. Sometimes things are a little blurry and twice now we've shown up in Kansas City, but in the wrong state. However, life on the road can be fun and I do use the word hesitantly because fun can really mess up my carefully laid plans. I think of the times I've sweated through the minutes. Whether I'm waiting for the caterer to arrive with the Green Tea or JB to return from 18 holes, the fun never stops. Even sprinting past Sunny at 8:00 am in the hotel lobby to get to the front desk first is rewarding at times. In fact, it's kind of fun writing this column. I guess my point is, no matter how silly the circumstances, it remains Widespread Panic. With that in mind, I will mention two topics which are unique to our show and could use some clarifying.
First, we have started a volunteer parking lot clean-up crew. I truly believe to continue our scene, we need to be accountable and responsible for the mess we make. The guidelines with the clean team are flexible to provide for different needs, but a few are constant. Please take note if you are interested:
1. Volunteers are recruited each show day. 2. The Clean boss will gather volunteers names usually an hour or more before the doors open. 3. Most of the job is pre-show; making sure trash bags are in the lots and people are using them, etc. 4. You are expected to help out after the show for general pick-up. Please remember we have to discourage people from using the Clean Team as a free ride for the tour.
The second topic is the band's taping policy. We currently can't provide official taper tickets for the shows, but again, a few guidelines are in place to help everyone. When the seating is general admission, I will designate an area directly behind the mix position. When the seating is reserved, you may tape from your purchased seat, as long as the mike stand is at head height. Remember to always listen to the venue staff as they are relaying my instructions to you and realize taping is a privilege, not a right.
All the talk of fun and sweat reminds me of a certain bandmember who we will call Oj-Oj. I believe the town was Los Angeles.... playing at the beautiful Palace Theater in Hollywood. I was going through my routine bandmember cross-check and came up one short. Turning to Trey I mumbled, Hermann is M.I.A. and we've got ten minutes until showtime. We instantly rushed out the stage door and headed for the street. Yes, this has happened before and instinctively we turned towards Hollywood Boulevard. After much radio-chatter and bar-searching,we located the missing link...
with guess who ?
Thanks for listening and always have a good time...
Dirk
Sense from the Smoker
I couldn't tell you how many times we have been asked to respond to the question, "Does Widespread Panic consider itself a 'southern' band?" Sloughing off the usual dinosaur band comparisons we inevitably respond with something like: "Well...we are from Georgia," or, "We really enjoy soul food." Soul food and Mom's Kitchen aside, one thing we all agree on is that we love barbeque. Even John Bell has been seen carefully shaping textured soy protein into little rib forms and covering them with sauce in his vegetarian days. I spent a few minutes talking with our resident drummer and smoked meat expert Todd Nance about the mystique of this age old southern tradition and how it applies to Widespread Panic.
"We have a real love for barbeque and soul food," states an enthusiastic Nance (whose step-father's business partner was a meat supplier), "It's a good way to spend your day. The effort and TLC you put into it is what makes the taste." It is this fondness for food made with love that often fuels the band itself. Todd takes us into the past for WP's first barbeque experience back in the days of the band's earliest out of town gigs in Macon, GA. "I guess it was around 1986 or so when we were doing weekends at the Rookery...Phillip (Walden, Jr.) took us to the Rib Shack and we ate barbeque right around the corner from where Duane and Berry crashed their motorcycles." All morbidity aside, it was love at first bite and thus began the band's quest for perfect barbeque that continues to this day.
"Around the same time we discovered Jimmy's in Lexington, NC by accident when we took an exit for gas," continues Nance, "And we still stop there when we can." In fact the tour bus made a stop on the way from Charlotte to Raleigh this spring. North Carolina is famous for it's barbeque and Jimmy's is no exception, although it is not THE most famous. "I guess it's kind of a sacrilage that we never tried to find Lexington #1," states Nance.
The next great step in our barbeque revolution was the discovery of Dreamland in Tuscaloosa, AL. "It was in 1991 right before we began making the Mom's Kitchen cd and we were finishing up a small tour with Blues Traveler," recalls Todd. " I remember sacrificing white bread to the ceiling fan...I don't think I can say that we out-ate them but we sure put some South in their mouth," laughs Nance.
"Barbeque lovers are like wine connoisseurs in a way...they all have their own tastes," says Nance. Apparently the style and preparation of barbeque varies regionally from the vinegar based pork tenderloin (served chopped or sliced) south down the coast from Virginia to Alabama to the heavier tomato sauce based beef brisket so common in Texas. "I like the vinegar based sauces," says Nance although Gomer has a bone to pick with him, liking the tomato based sauces more. Nance, a seasoned professional smoker, uses his ceramic kamado or "the egg" as he prefers to call it for his home barbeque needs. He recommends cooking the meat near or over an indirect fire and allowing it to tenderize in it's own juices. "This gives the meat a flavor based on it's natural juices and makes it so tender that you can just pull the meat away from the bone. That's why they call it 'pulled pork'."
When asked if there was a secret ingredient to making good barbeque, Todd simply replied, "You need patience and lots of beer," and smiling continued, "But most importantly gotta cook it with love."
Looking for some 'Que? Try these places!
Handy Andy, Oxford, Miss. Also an Exxon and a bait shop, this stuff is simply the best we have ever had. Ribs come wrapped in wax paper with Exxon logo on it. Thanks, Jojo!
Rib Shack, Macon GA. Strategically located near the scene of fatal crash scenes of Duane Allman and Barry Oakley. Ribs rule here!
Dreamland, Tuscaloosa and Birmingham, AL. Ribs done six ways: pork or beef, whole or half slab, and on white bread or with white bread.
Jimmy's, Lexington, NC. Last time we stopped we noticed that President Bill Clinton had recently posed for a photo with the owner.
Hog Heaven, Nashville, TN. On the recommend from Phil Walden we tried their smoked chicken with it's accompanying mysterious white sauce and have been hooked ever since. Had a large influence on the material recorded at Treasure Island Studio which eventually became the cd Everyday.
Rendezvous, Memphis, TN. Historic and hidden in an alley off of Union Street near the Peabody Hotel. Ribs. Period.
Tops, Memphis, TN. According to Todd, people laugh at him for this choice, but Sunny will back him up. Great pork sandwiches.
Arthur Bryant's, Kansas City, MO. Good barbeque but watch out for the double fried french fries!
Motel Hell
In
previous columns I have complained about clubs, stages, road trips, weather, artificial turf and other things that you the reader may not have been able to relate to personally. This column should remedy that problem because everybody's been to a bad motel. We, unfortunately, have many more chances at these encounters because of the amount of time we are on the road, but I doubt there is anyone out there who hasn't experienced the bad motel.
There are so many, I'm thinking of making this a recurring series.
Amazingly, some of my least favorite motels have also been the most expensive. Some of these motels actually call themselves hotels but I've never been able to tell the difference. Most of these places won't let you park your own car so you're always at the mercy of some valet if you need anything from your car, so you try to carry everything you'll need into the motel/hotel which draws a covey of bell boys so you now have to deal with them. Check-in seems to take forever while they search for a room that has been cleaned. You finally get to your expensive room where you find out they don't really have cable TV, just the networks, FOX, PBS, & a lot of Pay Per View movies. No Braves. You decide to look for the ice and drink machines, don't bother, you have to call room service for that. You shouldn't have to wait too long unless they're busy. Oh yeah, don't open that little hospitality cabinet/ refrigerator unless you want to double the price of your room. One more thing, don't forget to call the valet for your car way before you leave your room unless you want to be late or spend some time standing around in the lobby. I've seen all of this happen more than once. In fact, the last time I was in Colorado (Denver) all of the above happened plus the toilet ran over and the valet lost the keys to the rental car. I don't want to name names so all I'll say is it was a hotel.
In our early days on the road we would often stay six to a room, four band members and 2 crew. Some of us would sleep in the beds and the rest on sleeping bags on the floor. Your location would usually have more to do with who was snoring that night than it would with comfort. In those days almost all the motels we stayed at were bad. Usually they were also the cheapest ones available in that area so they were at least expected to be bad. Some of the place we stayed we were the only guests there not paying by the hour. Motels by airports, freeways, and other out of the way places were our homes away from home.
I remember Mike sleeping under the sink so he couldn't hear the TV. JB and I would usually sleep between, beside, or at the ends of the beds. Dave and Todd sometimes had to sleep in one bed. Of course the stars of these cheap motels are the housekeepers. I don't know where they find these people, but no morning is complete until you hear the knock at your door and the shrill cry of "housekeeping" over and over. These people are persistent if nothing else. I remember a housekeeper in Macon, GA making up Dave's bed one day while he was still asleep in the bed. OK we get the message, check-out time. I still cringe when I hear those words.
In general most of the places we've stayed on the road have been fine. But for those shining examples of inefficiency that we've all occasionally visited there's not much you can do about it but let off some steam and try to forget it ever happened. There, I feel better already.
Greetings from Brown Cat
Just a quick note
to thank Patrick Carter
for letting Moon Times use some of his artwork in this issue. All the funky colorful art you see in the latest issue is his. So much of the great artwork we use for all sorts of different things comes from fans who send in their own.
We are always looking for new artwork, so all you creative people out there send us everything you can to:
Widespread Panic/ Brown Cat, Inc.
2351 College Station Rd. Suite 504
Athens, GA 30605
I can't wait to see
everybody's stuff!!
Peace......Mary

issues.label: Vol. 3 #3
issues.title: Moon Times Vol. 3 #3
issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
Being part of the original Panic crew, taking care of the earliest mailing
lists among other things, Sara was the perfect choice to help Moontimes
reminisce about the old, old days.
Thanks Sara... Mary
Hardly seems like summer is over here in Athens and yet somehow Halloween is
right around the corner and we are already gearing up for New Years Eve.
Makes you realize how time flies when you are ready to Panic.
Dave was one of the first people I met in Athens. I was 17 and he was the
doorman at the Uptown. Our mutual love of freaky music made us instant
friends and we have remained so for all these years. When he asked me to
write something for Moontimes a few months ago it seemed like no problem. How
hard could it be to talk about the old days? Well, let me tell you, it's
amazing what comes back to you (and what doesn't) as one tries to remember
the details of running amok in youth. In search of inspiration, I dug through
some old bits and pieces from "back in the day". Before long I had the place
covered with old, tattered fliers, bar napkins with set lists on them
(complete with Larry's titles), some seriously wacky photos and piles of the
original mailing list cards. It only took a couple of canceled shows to come
up with "call before you haul".
I found all of these
little gems
to be a sweet reminder of how much fun
those days were. I can remember leaving my first show thinking all those
songs were WP originals. My punk roots had not left me at all prepared for
what was happening
on the stage and I began to see that Monday nights were
way too far apart (Thursday night bonus show - hooray!). I had nothing to
compare the experience to and it really opened my mind to a far greater
understanding of music and the power it can possess. It was so cool when the
boys started to hit the road, not just because we got more shows, but you
could really hear the difference in the playing as the band cut their teeth
night after night. Besides, there was just something extra crazy about shows
at places like Jackson Station (you had to be there). Before any of us knew
it, it seemed like we hardly got shows in Athens at all anymore.
There have been so many changes since then, at the time I guess I thought
the band was so good that it couldn't possibly get any better (that seems
pretty hilarious now). I knew it would get bigger, but I never thought I'd
hear
the boys jamming with Branford Marsalis and DJ Apollo or that I would
get swept up into the Fox Theater for yet another incredible 3 night stand.
At some point or another, we have all been part of a scene long enough to
get possessive of our experiences as part of that scene. I was certainly
guilty of this as the band moved into bigger venues like Center Stage and
Georgia Theater (even more hilarious). But soon I started to realize that the
crowds only made each show even more intense as hundreds, then thousands, of
people were all getting off just as hard as I was. This was a particularly
heavy moment for me. It was only then, and I know this to be even more true
today, that I saw the one thing that hadn't changed after all these years.
The magic in the house at a Panic show makes it impossible for anyone there
to seem like a stranger and no matter how many times I see it, I still want
more.
Hope to see you all out there soon!
- Sara Kelly Jones
Greetings from Brown Cat...
As premature as it may sound, Happy Holidays from Brown Cat!! This will be
the last issue of Moontimes for 1997, so this greeting covers pumpkins,
turkeys, and yuletide cheer. Halloween in New Orleans, Thanksgiving weekend
in San Francisco at the Warfield, and three nights at the Fox in Atlanta for
New Years gives new meaning to celebrating the holidays. We hope all of you
will come and celebrate with us.
It's been an incredible year so far and the next couple of months will close
out the year with a huge bang. We are already working on 1998 and I can
definitely say that the momentum isn't slowing down at all. The band will at
last be spreading some Panic in Europe and they plan to hit the road hard in
the good ole USA as soon as the jet lag wears off. The first issue of
Moontimes in 98 will tell all, so make sure we have your updated address. In
the mean time, you can keep up with what's going on by checking out our new
and improved web site at www.widespreadpanic.com. We are still in the process
of adding all sorts of new stuff so get on line and let us know what you
think. A big thanks to Brian Sofer at TUW Designs, Inc. for being the
ultimate webmaster.
We hope everyone has a fantastic holiday season. If you are hittin' the
road, be sure to be safe. I'll be checkin' in with you next year. Peace, Mary
Back to the Fox
We all had so much fun last year that the band couldn't pass up doing three
nights at the fabulous Fox Theater in Atlanta for New Years again this year,
December 29, 30, & 31. Mail order tickets are available through Brown Cat so
read the instructions very carefully and start making plans to ring in the
New Year with us.
