Widespread Panic / Brown Cat

issues.label: Vol. 3 #4
issues.title: Moon Times Vol. 3 #4
issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
Closing Out '97 and Right Into '98
We all had such a blast over New
Years in Atlanta - it just couldn't have been any better. The Fox was the perfect setting to close out 97 with a
bang and to jump right into 98. It was a total family affair complete with friends and family from far and
wide as well as a slide show to recap 1997.
One of the highlights of the shows was the first set of New Years Eve when
the band presented their own version of the progression and formation of
Widespread Panic. JB started off the set acoustic by himself for a few tunes
and then was joined by Michael, then Dave,
Todd, Sunny , and at the end JoJo.
Each night was complemented by special guests that came out to celebrate
with the band:
Tinsley Ellis is a well know guitar player from Atlanta
and one of the oldest friends of the band.
David Blackmon has been a
long time friend and occasional welcomed addition to Panic and can be seen
fiddlein' around the south with his current band the BTU's . The band met
Count Mbutu and
Rev. Jeff Mosier as players in the original Aquarium
Rescue Unit and have admired them ever since.
Eric Sanders and Chris
Ahler came out for a night to help out with drums.
John Keane took
some time out from the studio where he was working on the bands live album to
play a mean slide guitar on New Years Eve.
Randal Bramblett was in
Athens leading the Randal Bramblett Band when the band first met him and has
since had successful career playing with Sea Level as well as with Steve
Winwood and Traffic.
Col. Bruce Hampton what else do you need to know
about the Col. aside from he's the daddy of 'em all.
Light Fuse Get Away
To celebrate the release of the bands first live album, Widespread Panic will
be performing a free show for the album release party in their hometown of
Athens, GA on Saturday, April 18
The road to the release date of a live CD is a long one. The recording
process and archiving of the band's performances has always been a priority
at Brown Cat as well as on tour. The band owns seven Tascam ADAT recorders
and they are used every set, every night. The recording is done from the
monitor board on stage. Brad gets to hit record fourteen times each night
(...roll tape...) along with mixing the band's ears. The high quality
achieved in the recordings would not be possible without Brad and Tim's daily
routine of wiring and maintaining the machines. We have a proven system for
the task and we all work together to improve on it at every show. When the
tapes pile up on the road, we ship them back to the office. The number of
tapes is staggering and they are stored in a climate controlled "vault". The
vault would be a complete mess without Gomer's efforts to organize and sort
the tapes at the home office.
The band was careful in choosing the tracks for the CD and the selection
process was a challenge in itself. Once the song list was established, the
machines and tapes were sent to John Keane Studios for re-mixing and editing.
Without John Keane's and Billy Fields' hard work in the studio the project
wouldn't have been completed on schedule. Along with the band, John
carefully listened and then dialed each song into it's full potential. The
final product is very important to the band and nothing comes between the
highest quality sound and your ears.
We hope everyone enjoys the result of our efforts. The CD is the first
attempt in producing our live tapes and will not be the last. We know live
CD's are available and we cannot stop anyone from purchasing them. Thanks
to all of you who have written and informed us of who's selling what and
where. Brown Cat warns the quality of "bootleg" CD's is sub-standard and
these are not authorized in any way. Tape trading and audience tapes are
great vehicles to carry the music, however the recent increase in unofficial
CD's seems to question, how far can it go? We encourage you to take our
new CD for a ride and hear what we're talking about. Just be sure you keep
it loud...
Copyright © 2001
Widespread Panic / Brown Cat

issues.label: Vol. 4 #1
issues.title: Moon Times Vol. 4 #1
issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
We knew it was going to be big but we had no idea just how big it would be. Estimates ranged from 70,000 to over 100,000 people in attendance at the Light Fuse, Get Away CD release party in downtown Athens, GA April 18th. From our point of view it all looked like crowded sweaty fun and we can only say that it was a thrill to do what we do best in front of so many enthusiastic folks. But before we start patting ourselves on the back there are other folks we must commend because without their persistence we would never have overcome the hurdles that inevitably pop up when trying to arrange for a production of this size. To our Mayor, Gwen O'Looney and ADDA Executive Director Art Jackson we give high praise for getting the ball rolling and making sure through pure diligence and will alone that it kept rolling. To the Athens Police Department we give our praise for being such a pleasant and peaceful presence (not to mention actually enjoying the show). If it were not for the midnight hour efforts of Capricorn Records this event could have easily slipped through our fingers. To the city of Macon GA we give thanks for offering their beautiful city when it looked like the Athens location might not happen. As far as the efforts of the entire Brown Cat staff we can only quote band manager Sam Lanier who said when it was all over, "I have never been more proud of our staff... They all contributed 200%!" And finally, the largest debt of gratitude goes to all in attendance for behaving in such a wonderful manner. Not only did you impress the local city council and merchants but also the entire community with your cheerful spirit of being there to simply have fun enjoying the music. We want to make sure that you give yourselves a big hand because due to your generosity in our can drive to support the Northeast Georgia Food Bank you provided 1.5 tons of food and over $500 cash. To top it off, one anonymous donor who could not attend the concert sent 150 pounds of plucked poultry after calling the Food Bank and finding out that what they needed most was meat. This is the kind of behavior we can all be proud of and with this in mind we thank you all for helping us set the world record for the biggest album release party (topping Metallica.... whoa!) and for having a great time in our Classic City of Athens... Dave
Brown Cat could fill up an entire Moontimes with thank you's to the many, many people who truly made April 18th happen. Aside from those saints mentioned above we would love to thank: The University of Georgia, Athens Regional Medical Center, all the volunteers and the Convention & Visitors Bureau, the Unified Government of Athens-Clarke County, Eastern Stage Productions, Lighting Technologies, Inc., the 40 Watt, 103.7 WPUP, Rock Solid, Simone Giannini and Buck Williams. It was stunning to see our entire community come together to pull off such an incredible event. We also had
a wonderful, diverse group of sponsors that no doubt we could not have lived without.
Thank you... Mary
Copyright © 2001
Widespread Panic / Brown Cat

issues.label: Vol. 4 #2
issues.title: Moon Times Vol. 4 #2
issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
This summer has been nothing short of outrageous. While we've been sweating our butt's off here in Athens, the band has been around the world and seen it all. Canvassing the band and crew to see what the summer's highpoints were, brought on a barrage of stories and special moments. What seemed to be the most common denominator in all conversations was the fact that this summer's Widespread Panic and Friends tour quickly became Widespread Panic and Co-conspirators. All the hard work that a tour is aside, it sounded a little like summer camp out there. Everyone raved about how much fun they had hanging out with the other bands on the tour.
G-Love and Special Sauce was in for the long haul for the whole tour. (Quote from G-Love)
Any of the 10's of thousands of you who made it out to the show in Athens this past Spring more than likely noticed a bunch of video cameras around. Whether they were wandering through the streets, propped up on platforms, or swinging from a crane, the fact that there was a lot of filming going on was hard to miss. Panic In The Streets is what the band has decided to call the finished product and once you see it you'll know why. I, personally, never made it out from backstage that night, so to see the footage of the crowd was mind blowing. We kept getting reports throughout the night on how big the crowd was and later got the numbers, but we couldn't have imagined what it looked like until we saw the film. So, the band picked out their favorite parts of the video and the editing process began. While the director was busy cutting up film, the audio was sent over to John Keane to ensure the best sound possible. Four songs of that excellent audio has also been transferred to a CD that will be included with the video package so you can pop it into your stereo for maximum listening pleasure. So October 6th, be on the look out for Painc In The Streets at your local record store. If you were there, you won't believe you were there, and who knows, you just might see yourself. If you weren't there, now you will be.
We were lucky enough to work with some really great people in the production of the video. The director, Chris Weinstein from With A Twist Productions, came all the way from New York to hang out with us for days to get to know the band and Athens. He worked hard to make this special show into a special video and I thought you guys would enjoy hearing his perspective of the most organized chaos Athens has ever seen.
Just added to Panic's calendar is a show in Crested Butte, CO in between summer and fall tours. The One World Music Festival is dedicated to environmental and cultural preservation and has been presenting great music for great causes for seven years. Profits from this year's festival will go toward the fight to stop the Cyprus-Amax Mining company from opening a molybdenum mine on the prominent Mt. Emmond, a.k.a. "the Red Lady", in Crested Butte. The festival will take place over Labor Day Weekend, September 5, 6, & 7. The three day passes for $85 (sorry no one day passes available) will get you a fantastic weekend of music including The Robert Cray Band, Merl Saunders and The Rainforest Band, The original "P" - Parliaments, and Galactic along with Widespread Panic. Panic will close out the weekend on Monday night to ensure you go home completely exhausted. For more information about The One World Music Festival (exact location and directions, band line-up, etc.) you can check out their website at http://www.oneworldmusic.org #or to go ahead and order tickets call 888 346-7293. Parking is limited so everyone should try and travel together. There is a campsite near by, as well as some of the best single-track bike trails anywhere. So pack it up, bring your friends and head to one of the most beautiful places in the US for a long weekend with some of the best music around.
As hard as we work here at Brown Cat, it is impossible to ignore how lucky we are to have a fan base that works for the band as well. So much of Widespread Panic's success is the result of people whose lives have been touched by the music in such a positive way that they take it upon themselves to spread the word about this band. Aspects of the Panic experience such as the massive tape trading of live shows, An Honest Tune (with their magazine, updates, and Drivin' flyers), and The Everyday Companion do so much to let other people know what Widespread is all about. With the band's dedicated fan base in mind, we want to try and recruit some additional help in the promotion of the concerts. What we are looking for are serious and motivated people to help distribute promotional materials in their own town when the band comes through. Compensation for efforts would be tickets and passes to the show and a great big thank you from Brown Cat. If you are interested in taking on this mission (very serious inquiries only), send us your name, address, phone #, and why you would you are the right person to do this in your town. Send it to:
Panic Promotion Department
400 Foundry St.
Athens, GA 30605
You have always made a difference before and we know you will with this.
Copyright © 2001
Widespread Panic / Brown Cat

issues.label: Vol. 4 #3
issues.title: Moon Times Vol. 4 #3
issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
So, here at Brown Cat we are gearing up for fall and the holidays in a big way. We've just gotten back from Memphis where the band did two nights at Mud Island, the first trip of many this Fall, and the energy at the shows was just incredible. The momentum going toward another spectacular holiday season is building fast. Other trips for us will include, among others, New Orleans for Halloween and, of course, Thanksgiving in Chicago. Then, it will definitely take a month to recover and get ready for yet another run of four nights at The Fabulous Fox Theater in Atlanta, GA for New Years, December 28th - 31st. Celebrating the holiday season with "the Panic family", the shows and atmosphere that go along with that, has become a tradition we look forward to every year. Fans travel from far and wide to experience the holiday extravaganza with special friends and great music. It's a feeling we wish we could bottle and sell the rest of the year.
We know many of you are wondering what happened with Panic in the Streets, the video from theLight Fuse Get Away album release party here in Athens last Spring. There were some problems in the production process, so most of the videos were recalled.
The new release date is November 3rd. We know some of you did get the first release, but if you would like to trade it in for one that has everything it is supposed to have, just send it to Brown Cat (the address is on the back) and we'll send you a new one. It turned out so great and is a wonderful representation of what happened that day in Athens!! Mayor O'Looney with her,"Greeeeetttttiiiiiiiinnnngggs", exhibits her fabulous spirit so you can understand how lucky we were to work with her in making the show happen. She was even generous enough recently to present the band with keys to the city for their unwavering commitment to giving such a gift to Athens.
In other news, the band was very excited this month to be able to participate in an American Library Association campaign targeted toward teens. The ALA dubbed October 19th - 25th TEEN READ WEEK and have Widespread Panic as their ambassadors of books in the slightly off the wall
teen READ poster . Aside from the books they have in the poster, their books of choice from childhood are: JB - The Helen Keller Story, Dave - The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn, Sunny - Swiss Family Robinson, Todd - Johnny Tremain, Mike - The Hobbit, and Jo Jo - Roberto Clamente. Speaking of kids, the Ortiz family has a new addition. Anna Grace Ortiz was born August 20th and she is absolutely adorable. Congratulations Kelly and Sunny!!
With things moving along at what seems like an ever increasing pace for the band, Brown Cat wants to make sure you guys are as informed on what is happening as efficiently as possible. With Moontimes going out only 4 times a year, it is hard to be able to include everything that might come up in the coming months; such as mail order for Chicago.
By the time you get this moontimes, the deadline for mail order for Chicago will be over, but New Years mail order information is inside. Thus, the web site has become such a valuable avenue for up-to-date information. We know that not everyone is on-line so we are posting all sorts of information on what we are calling the Panic Board located at the merch stand at concerts. Make sure you check it out when you get to the shows so you know what's going on in between Moontimes issues. We have also made up mail order cards (3 X 5 cards with all information on them that you just fill out and send in) for what ever shows we are providing mail order for. Just pick them up at the merch stand and you're on your way.
We also would like to thank all of you who offered to help Brown Cat with the promotion of shows. We have gotten a wonderful response. Keep 'em coming. We are keeping them all on file and will get in touch with you when we are doing promotion in your town. It sure means a lot to Brown Cat that so many are willing to put forth their own efforts to spread the word about Panic. Thank you. Well, that's about it for now. We've still got over a month of touring left including all the holiday hoopla. I can't believe I'm saying this again already, but I'll check in with you guys next year!!
Peace......Mary
What 'Cha Wanna Know?
a little Q & A
Landon asked JB:
When you are on tour, do you and the other band members pay attention to remaining injury free. That is, do you stay away from basketball, arm wrestling, and boxing so that you don't break any fingers or hurt yourself
some other way? I am curious. I think I would most likely play it safe.
Dear Landon,
When I was in Junior High, I broke my leg in a football game. The team we were playing was supposed to be huge and mean - and I went into that game worried about getting crushed - and that's what happened. I remember feeling that if I had gone into the game with less fear and more open-eyed confidence, I'd have had a lot more fun, and been of more value to the team.
The last thing I want to do on the road - or at home for that matter - is screw up in such a way that I wouldn't be able to carry my load as a Band member. At the same time, I don't want to shy away from all the fun things there are to do in life to protect doing one thing in life. If I did, I wouldn't have a well-rounded life experience to fuel my contribution to the songwriting process of Widespread Panic, and
Oops... there I'd be - not carrying my load as a Band member.
So, my only choice seems to be to engage in life with as much attention and intention as I can muster, be realistic with my limits, and be willing to stretch those limits when I've gained enough experience to
move ahead safely.
In summation: Being patiently intense is cool, drinking beer while using power tools is not cool.
Nick B. asked Sunny:
What are your influences in music and what style do you consider your playing?
The band Tower of Power got me into congas in 1971. Of course I'd always listen to Santana, Ray Barritto, Willie Bobo, Mungo Santamaria, and Tito Puente. All may have lots to do with the style I play. Mostly, I play to add color in music and compliment what is being heard.
Jellybean asked Dave:
Fleeter upton transoopton' himble?
..um... Paul is dead?
Pat Leo asked Sunny:
If you were a drum, what type of drum would you be?
I would be a belly drum. I have added a few pounds.
Andrew Osborne asked JoJo:
I read in the Memphis Flyer that you raise Bush Hogs to eat the Kudzu in your yard, and then cook'em up whey they get fat! How do you cook them?
Andrew
You get the prize for the best question. Thanx. I could probably write a book on the culinary delights of bush hogs. In the north Mississippi hills, many prefer a vinegar-based DW40 to marinate the bush hog before barbequing. Of course, the most important thing to remember is to make sure your bush hog is not running as you baste it. Oh and, of course, SAVE THE LIVERS!!!
Scotty Wright asked Mike:
If you had the chance to see any great guitar player who had ever played perform a life show, but had to choose just one, who would it be?
Steve Howe from YES
Here is one more from JB....
Within the music industry, who do you consider to be the the greatest lyricist(s) of all time, and why?
Without a doubt, it's a group called the ATTORNEYS from New York City; their lyrics can mean so many different things all at the same time.
Someone asked Mike:
Who does your hair?
My Mom
Mitch Mitchell asked Todd:
What have been some of your most memorable drum segments involving guests with WSP?
Hey Mitch
One of my most memorable segments would be Boston 97 with DJ Apollo?
Adam K. asked JoJo:
What musician/band has influenced you the most and why?
Hey Adam
Well John Lennon and Neil Young because their songs have meant the most to me throughout my life. But it was Professor Longhair that got me stated on the piano and playing in bands. None could turn
blues to happiness like 'Fess. Like he always said, "It's my own fault for coming home from work early last night"
Thanx Adam and God Bless You, JoJo
Austin Lilling asked Dave:
I would like to ask Schools, if you were on a deserted island with only a beer and a discman, what would the beer be and what would the three discs you would take with you be?
Austin,
I don't drink beer; can I take an extra disc to the island?
Miles Davis; Get Up With It
DJ Shadow; Endtroducing
REM; Document
Radiohead; OK Computer
Ashton Hargrave asked Todd:
What is your favorite kind of beer?
Cold!

Widespread Panic / Brown Cat

issues.label: Vol. 5 #1
issues.title: Moon Times Vol. 5 #1
issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
From the Studio
Greetings and welcome to the first issue of MOONTIMES for 1999. It seems like only a few short days ago that we were rocking the Fabulous Fox Theater in Atlanta and ringing in the last New Year of the nineties. In reality, we have been sequestered at Keane Recording, Ltd. since January 11 working diligently on the yet untitled new album.
John Keane is producing once again, only this time he is taking a more "hands- on" approach. In other words, he is sitting back and letting the capable Bradshaw Leigh (who has worked with Tracy Chapman) twist knobs and push faders while he conceives ideas for new sounds and arrangements. As usual, he managed to squeeze a banjo performance of his own into one track. We are all really happy with this new approach to recording from John and we think you will recognize and enjoy the difference as much as we have. The amazing thing about this session was the incredible swiftness with which tracks were recorded. Nearly twenty songs were cut in six days. Thanks to the amazing accuracy of
Todd's drumming performances we were able to spend much more time on arrangements and new sounds than in the past. In fact, you will hear a song or two that you have never heard before in addition to some formally dressed arrangements of tunes that have become familiar over the last twelve months. I think you will agree that everyone really performed in an exceptional manner for this recording and that there is a multitude of emotions realized on this disc. I myself have laughed, cried, and broken out in goosebumps just from listening to the rough mixes. This can usually be considered a good sign. In addition to exciting performances from us, you will also be treated to several guests who were kind enough to lend their talent to this project. Colin Butler of Big Ass Truck scratches some turntables, Anne Richmond Boston of Swimming Pool Cues helps out with background vocals, The Dirty Dozen Horns make an appearance, and we were really blown away by the uplifting performance turned in by Dottie Peoples and The People's Choice Chorale. I will leave it up to your imagination to figure out the where and how of these guest performances.
Believe me, there is nothing I would rather tell you than the song titles and even the sequence of them on the disc, but I wouldn't want to spoil the surprise. But don't worry, there will be one more issue of MOONTIMES before the July release of the new CD. Perhaps I will have changed my mind in reference to the divulgence of the songs by then. But don't hold your breath! I would also love to give you a title to hold on to, but it seems, for us, coming up with a good title is harder than coming up with new material (and we wouldn't have it any other way). I can tell you that we have brought back the amazing visual talent of J. Flournoy Holmes to produce the CD cover. For our efforts we benefited from yet another first: artwork that simply floored all of us. If you thought that Flournoy's past covers (Allman Bros. Eat A Peach and our own Space Wrangler to name only a few) were great, just wait until you see this one. I guarantee that you won't be able to put it down because we weren't either, and we are the hardest sell of all. So, I have basically told you nothing about our new CD except that it will be out July 27 courtesy of Capricorn records. I hope you are as excited as we are for this newbie to hit the streets. Mark your calendars and read on, because inside you will find all the spring tour information we can give you. Enjoy! -
Dave
Leaving John's studio for the night I feel like Grandpa Munster closing the trap door from his smokey lab: Kind of giddy from the process, but not actually knowing what form Herman(n) will assume by the next morning. So far, we have about seventeen songs we are working from to make up this album. Some of 'em you've heard a lot, so we're going to change 'em. Some of 'em are brand new so we don't know them well enough to ask them to change, and some of them are complete rip-offs of other people's songs - so we have to change 'em. We've known John Keane since '87 - and every time we begin a new project, we get new knobs to look at. Some knobs we get to play with, and sometimes some of those are a part of something that's plugged in. We get to play with his cat too. This much truth I can give you - there's a lot of cool stuff you can do with your buddies in the studio (beyond ordering food and watching TV) and so far it looks like we're experimenting more with those possibilities. Then again, it's easy to go over the line by attending more to the various knobs than the motion of music. Maybe we should call the album With All Our Heart, + Some Knobs. I'm sure we'll do more live albums in the future as we improve on the live recording process - giving us more freedom to follow the music into more make-believe realms while in the studio. -
JB
In the life of a band there are few things that mark the chapters of its existence better than the albums it creates. I was fortunate enough to have been around the hallowed walls of John Keane's Studio in Athens for a few days while the boys in the band created their newest chapter. Number one rule in the studio is, "Get used to sitting around." As I'm sure many of you know, sitting around breeds some rather decadent behavior. Widespread Panic has perfected the art of enjoying Nutritious Snacks to make the time go by quicker. Here are the Band Member's Favorite Snack Foods: Jojo- "Jolly Green Giant" Greenbean Casserole Dave- "Hershey's" Chocolate and Toffee Nuggets Todd - World Famous Taco Stand Delicacies JB- "GO GO" All Natural Super Drinks Sunny- "Ginger Snaps" ( A Fat Free Snack) Mikey - Budweiser and Fish Sandwiches (Another Day, Another Fish Sandwich) Listening to Widespread Panic record an album could go from quite humorous to totally inspiring. One minute they'd be plowing their way through mistakes and the next they would be effortlessly breezing towards the takes you'll hear on the album. All in all my favorite memory of hanging around the studio is hearing the relaxed laughter of the band and some friends one night after the work had almost ended. Everyone seemed so pleased with the day's work. I think this record will reflect what a good time the boys and Mr. Keane had making it. And don't forget to enjoy some snacks while enjoying it yourself !!!! See ya on Tour. - Peter Jackson
The Inside Word
What does it mean to be a
Widespread Panic Crew member? Depends on the day you ask . When we're at some beautiful outdoor venue and the weather is cooperating, we'd say life is pretty good. Or when we're at some miserable parking lot/venue frying like eggs in the summer heat and none of us have had showers in several days, we'd say that life on the road is not fit for any human life form. It's just like anything else, we have bad days and good days. But...there is always the gratification of seeing the band get on stage and make all of you guys happy. Here are a few items we would like to share with you to give you some insight into our daily lives out there. Enjoy...

issues.text:
Falling into the 19th Hole
Wednesday, May 5, 1999, marked the occasion of the first ever
Widespread Panic/House of Blues golf tournament in Myrtle Beach, SC. Held at the Arcadian Shores Club, 32 golfers, all legends in their own minds, convened in
competition for the coveted trophy. Scrambled into
4-man teams , the House of Blues contributed twelve strong players culled from the nightclub staff while Team Panic featured twenty friends, crew, and band members referring to
themselves as "professional hackers." Chris Edwards, John Bostwick, Drew Scott, and lighting director Brian "Brain" Cohn made up the winning foursome. Looking at that team it’s no wonder that a beer cart was not to be found anywhere! Awards were given in other categories as well: closest to the hole (Wilson Patrick), longest drive (Jason Plassacar), and most lost balls (John Bostwick with a wasteful total of eight). My team came in last with a three over par. John Bell, Todd Nance, and our main man behind the wheel of the semi, Rich Beedle each
contributed to this dubious effort. Thanks go to Jim Mallonee, Tracy Webber, and Kathy Bishop from the House of Blues for making this beginning tradition happen. Keep ‘em long and straight and I’ll see you on the links.
- Trey
Dirtzy Dozen Brass Band All Summer Long
Think back to Halloween 1998. If you were in New Orleans with Panic
celebrating the holiday cajun style, you got to check out the
Dirty Dozen Brass Band opening the show and sitting in on several songs including "Swamp," "Red Hot Mama," and "Superstition." For those of you who couldn’t be with us, you will have all summer to not only catch the Dozen opening up our summer shows, but sitting in with the band every night as well. Please come early, as the Dozen will allow taping.
If you’re not familiar with DDBB, pick up their latest release, "Buck Jump." Classic brass band sounds are joined with intricate jazz/funk while showcasing the production talents of John Medeski of Medeski, Martin and Wood fame. Medeski’s stamp is undeniable, both in the overall spontaneous live feeling of the recording and more directly with his soulful Hammond B3 playing. For those who need to be convinced, no problem, they’ll be performing cuts from the new album and selling CDs at all shows. In addition to their own new album, DDBB will appear on the new Widespread album. Past credits include guest spots on the last two Black Crowes records and collaborations with everyone from Elvis Costello to Dr. John.
For more info, audio and video footage and contests, go to
www.mammoth.com.
Big Manch Daddy
It’s been a few years since this newsletter you now hold began as a black and white broadsheet with about 1,000 Kinko’s copies being circulated around the country to Panic fans. Over those years I have attempted to bring you not only "official" news and opinions, but also some "fan appreciation" on
occasion. I first discovered the talents of Big Maunch Daddy when perusing the Spreadnet archives
webpage desperately seeking some tidbit of factual trivia that could keep the band from repeating the exact same setlist in some nameless city in which we had performed a year before. I was instantly hooked on his casual yet educated use of verbiage. Being the laziest of all possible editors (it’s true, just ask Mary) I contacted him in hopes of persuading him to take some heat off of my overused brain by writing a small piece on his view of the "Panic scene."
What follows is a horribly cut up and rearranged
version (due only to space restrictions) of what
"the Mauncher" sent to me. Read on and enjoy, but remember that the opinions expressed belong solely to him.
- Dave
Somebody contacted me about writing a piece on some aspect of "the Panic Scene." I fired off, "Pretty broad brush y’got there," or some such glib reply, in disbelief that my grainy snapshots could be considered amusing for the public at large. Shortly thereafter, I realized that when you try to write about something that is so close to you all the time, you hardly know where to begin.
I began compiling a list of things I knew I’d need...sound bytes from friends and relatives, paints, brushes & bolts of canvas, a stolen hotel towel set, a Zippo, inconspicuous parcels of "inspiration" forwarded to the various locales I’d be sure to visit, a couple of cases of Maker’s Mark, one of those atlases that shows all the paved and unpaved roads, some new-to-me tapes, chest waders (for those countless hours of conversation with the various hustlers, hooligans, name droppers, crazies, and other wild mannered hell raisers I was sure to encounter), and some sows’ ears. A large brooding American car that had plenty of room to stretch out, cozied with a certain radiant fear factor (an old yellow cab crossed my mind). A whole spring tour’s booking of tickets, lodging, and meals...we’re talking honest meals, too. Plus, I’d need a sack full of money or at the very least a large loosely verified expense account with which to fend off and otherwise survive my travails.
In my more paranoid moments, I began to wonder if there wasn’t some conspiracy afoot to keep my brain full of unrecordable thoughts and have me implode by way of mentally constipated silence. "Man, that oughta be a cakewalk...just write what you know." And this from my landlord: advice that was something akin to "There is no try...only do." Thanks, chief...are you on the payroll too? Such witticisms from my traveling companions...lotsa laughs all right, mostly at my expense.
Undaunted and cautiously optimistic, I realized I had been seeing the Panic on and off for, let’s see ... ten, maybe twelve years now? During that time, the band and I both passed through many moons on many different worlds...and not always hand in hand. If there was any prerequisite for being able to riff on "the scene as I saw it," clearly, I had placed way out of it. That gave me a good dose of perspective which hardened off my backbone. When my life was at it’s most hectic and I was running myself ragged, my Panic tapes were safe haven from the noise and haste.
I think "the scene" is whatever you make of it. When we’re not on our way to, at, or coming from a Widespread Panic concert, we get crystal clear reception when we tune in the band’s frequency and realize we’re living in the "golden age of Panic."
The scene is a personal mind set that we fabricate ourselves. We project our personal expectations, dreams, energies and more at live events (sometimes we project other stuff too, but that’s a different colored horse). It is this composite of everyone present and their experiences (including the band’s) that makes being in that moment one of the most elusive places to be in this universe.
I’ve had some truly shining moments during the consciousness expanding experience I refer to as "seeing the Panic." Between the crowd, the buzz, the band, and the set list, it’s one movie screen-sized tapestry that gently undulates as you wander through its velvety folds. But being in the moment, while nicely dressed when she comes around, is kinda fair weather as moments go.
You struggle to bring a corner home with you...some small tatter that no one will ever miss. You share your remnant with your friends, swearing it put the "super" in superlative; best damn thing you’ve seen...ever. Your mind tempers those memories, the same way those halfway awake dreams between the first and second snooze button get watered down shortly after you splash some cold water on your face. Strangely vivid and kinda incoherent, they end up pleasantly familiar but bewildering.
Most of the time, I listen to my tapes for the sheer audio joy they’re stuffed with; but my ramshackle tape collection is worth far and away more to me than my sketchy recollections of shows in terms of the lucidity, pleasure, and creativity bequeathed by the Panic insight, moods, and imagery. I can remember those experiences, some of those elusive details and transient impulses each time I pop in one of my tapes and try to recapture those half-waking dreams. My efforts at spreading the music around are a direct manifestation of this so-called "pleasure effect." I feel compelled to share these vignettes with someone, anyone...just so somebody else can feel the way that tape makes me feel.
That’s just me. While I might be one loquacious king hell Panic freak, there are others like me out there that aren’t as verbose. We might not appear to have a lot in common on the surface, but there is one thing we share, right below our veneers: the way we’ve all incorporated the Panic into our lives, experiences, relationships, and daily routines.
You feel like the Panic has become your close friend. You imagine them smiling down at you, perhaps peering over their rock n’ roll shades, into your life...your confidants that know how it really is with you. It just doesn’t matter, despite its one sided intimacy.
"Show me something new," you quip.
Okay...try this: Don’t depend on the live experience as your soul source of Panic communion.
Don’t chalk me up to "Sounds dubious" straight off. Like my ranting is gonna slow you down. The irony that you’re taking the time to read this is not lost on me, to be sure. I’m not knocking live shows; I hardly expect the band to endorse everyone staying at home for obvious reasons.
I’ve got some friends who went to see the Panic on their first date together back in '92. They’re set to marry later this spring. It’s no coincidence that the festivities are close to the time when Panic will be passing through our little backwater.
I know one boy who works on antebellum houses repairing sunbaked slate roofs. He parks that beat up, dropped-off-the-side-a-couple-of-times tape player in the shade of the chimney and ties up that sweatband waiting for "Pigeons" to come warbling out of the taped-in-place tweeters.
Then there’s this law student who leaves a picture of JB, resplendent in rooster headgear, on all the terminals he uses in the stodgy ol’ law library. Raises a few eyebrows among the less macrobiotic, I’m sure.
These are the kind of scenes I’m talking about: any "away-from-the-shows" scene.
These days, my companions and I have found the Panic live viewing experience to be a great way to vacation and see good friends...close, closer, and, finally, from some distance. With a little forethought, and some competent buzz planning, a real life "Heaven" can be yours for the low-low price of twenty-five bucks a pop.
Cherish your shows and large Panic experiences. Potent as they may be, if you get your mojo right by fine tuning that soundtrack and keeping your movie focused, you’ll find shows will stand out above all others as "your favorite time."
But don’t take it from me. See for yourselves. Keep these musings on the tip of your tongue...it’ll keep that spine in line.
- Big Manch Daddy

Widespread Panic / Brown Cat

issues.label: Vol. 5 #3
issues.title: Moon Times Vol. 5 #3
issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
HELLO FROM THE BIG APPLE!!!!
Where to start about the best part of this trip. It's pretty much been one big best part. I LOVE THIS TOWN!! The energy here is unbelievable and perfect for these shows. A big whompper of two nights in New York City at Roseland, the release of 'Til The Medicine Takes, as well as the grand finale of Summer Tour. Any one of these would have been enough love for an evening or so.
It's Thursday and I'm whipped. It's been non-stop since Tuesday when Capricorn (the band's record company) threw a great party to celebrate the release of the new record. It was at a neat little place on the upper west side called the Delta Grill. I'm usually really bad about taking pictures, but we remembered to grab an always invaluable disposable camera and blinded everyone so we could share some moments with you guys. Thanks to Capricorn for a great time!
The music was over the top. We'll have some
pictures from the shows by the time you get this on the BRAND NEWLY DESIGNED website (thanks to Brian Sofer) that everyone should check out. The only picture that I really wish I had was one of Panic and Dirty Dozen saying goodbye to each other on stage at the end of the
concert on Wednesday. After the Dozen closed the night with the band, I thought they would all hug for 20 minutes. Made me all teary. A fantastic relationship was born on Summer Tour between the two bands and we look forward to more Dirty Dozen Brass Band in Panicland in the future.
With the record finally out, the band goes home for a week before heading to Europe. Then they'll be back on the road in the US for fall. This tour has been great, I can't believe it's over. From Mud Island to Red Rocks to Roseland, thanks for an incredible summer.
See ya back in Georgia.
Love, Mary
Copyright © 2001
Widespread Panic / Brown Cat

issues.text:
TWAS THE NIGHTS BEFORE NEW YEARS....
‘Twas the nights before New Years, when all through the town
Not a creature was stirring except for a cat colored brown
The tickets were packaged and mailed out with care
In hopes that all the orders would make it out there
Fall tour was ending, it had been quite a haul
Beginning at Oak Mountain proved much fun for all
Then cross the country through Austin and San Fran
Back to New Orleans and ending in Virginia, by damn!
Home in Athens, excitement was churning,
Mary,
Ellie , and Greta keep the home fires burning
With Sam at the Brown Cat helm, keeping a watchful eye
The holiday season approaches and is sure to fly by
Outside it is beginning to get as cold as ice
So Richard, Gomer, and Crumpy are busy sending out merchandise
Whether it’s a fleece, a t-shirt, or a hat you desire
Just give them a ring and they’ll send your attire
What is next in this land of music you might ask
Topping four nights at The Fox will prove quite a task
With a stage being built and bright lights being hung
Phillips Arena is where Panic New Years will be rung
All of Panic land lies waiting to spin and twirl
With visions of a Chilly or a Fishwater whirl
It won’t be long now, the answers to the chatter
Listen very closely, you can hear the clatter
Down the road comes a tour bus with a glow and a gleam
With Wilson on board, it is sure to be “green” clean
Draper will see that all are fed just right
With Bubba & Yogi at the reigns, they fly safe through the night
Now Trey, now Peter, now Garrie and Wayne
On Walko, on Danny, on Brad and Brain
Wait, there’s Gardner, Simon, Billy, and Jason
Crew load away, load away, load away with hasten
And then, in a twinkling, they appear out of the mist
JR driving a six headed machine to give 2000 a new twist
Mike begins with that long lingering lead
While Dave lays down that bass we need
Todd keeps things solid and moving at the same time
And with Sunny around you will hear bells chime
JB’s words and melody are sure to please
Everyone is ready, but where is JoJo with the keys?
All at once the music will grabs it’s groove
And the sea of smiling faces begins to sway and move
Before the night is over, all will become one you see
It’s a matter of giving, from me to you and you to me
So keep yourselves safe and spread lots of cheer
We hope you are snug with your loved ones near
We’ll all be together soon, as the future looks bright
Happy holidays to all and to all a good night.
Copyright © 2001
Widespread Panic / Brown Cat

issues.label: Vol. 6 #1
issues.title: Moon Times Vol. 6 #1
issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
Welcome to this first issue of 2000
As you can see, we went for a new look this issue. This was done for a few reasons: sometimes change is good and this volume will be the last chance we get to have a little fun with the design of Moontimes. Moontimes will be joining the modern, cyber world by the end of 2000. With everything around the band growing furiously and the Moontimes mailing list exploding to immense proportions, we are going to take the opportunity to provide a more interactive approach for the newsletter on the internet. This will expand the possibilities for Moontimes in ways we are very excited about. So, at some point this year, you will have to make sure we have your current e-mail address. The easiest way being via the website, news and information/updates section. There is a page there to enter your email address to be notified of big news, web updates, and eventually the new Moontimes. Again, this won't happen until the end of the year, but we wanted to give everyone as much advance notice as possible. It's a good idea to go ahead and give us your email address anyway so we can let you know what is happening the rest of the year.
It's been quiet around Brown Cat for the past couple of months. Time for everybody to do something a little different. Lots of stories inside. Spring Tour information is all mapped out for you in it's usual spot. As usual, further information, tons of links, and any changes will be posted on the website and hotline as soon as we know about it. The band is extra excited to be starting spring tour here in Athens at the beautiful Classic Center Theater for three nights. The last time the band played here was in 1995 for Halloween when they did two shows at the Classic Center Exhibit Hall. The Theater wasn't finished yet back then, but now they get to take advantage of one of the best sounding rooms in Georgia. These shows will be special in many ways including one that the band wanted to send a special message out about:
Not an April Fool's joke: The members of the Band respectfully request that the Athens shows be attended without taping gear. That is our wish just for those nights - The rest of the tour will be business as usual. Please know, we hold the craft and sport of taping by audience members in high regard.
We'd be grateful if you trust us on this and leave
all your recording gear behind. Dancing is
still encouraged.
So, with that said, put your touring boots on and we'll see you on the road.
Knobs, Buttons and Blinking Lights
Among other fun, winter camp activities
mentioned inside, there is big news from the tape vault of 1999. The band has been spending time in the studio with John Keane to cull the best of the best of the band's shows with The Dirty Dozen Brass Band in 1999. Guaranteed to be down right funky "dirty", this latest musical
wonder will be available through Widespread merchandise later on
this spring.
New Years Eve 1999-2000
Greetings from the Production Office of the Widespread Panic World Headquarters.
As an incredibly large number of you know (Over 15,000 per night!).... The Philips Arena Shows brought in 2000 in a truly grand fashion. The band and crew orchestrated the biggest indoor concerts in the history of Widespread Panic. Everyone put forth such a huge effort,
planning for basically the entire year
prior to produce these shows.
The Crew got to play with some exciting new showbiz toys. Pyrotechnics and Laser technologies were used for the first time, more sound than we have ever used... over 270,000 watts of power covering 270 degrees of the arena, 3 tractor trailers of nothing but lights, and even a disco ball.
The Band stuck to some more tried and true methods to enhance some of the most powerful Panic ever. Two words folks...
Dottie Peoples . The Atlanta based Gospel singer brought all of the enormous power of Gospel music to Widespread Panic and The Dirty Dozen Brass Band brought all of their class and New Orleans style funk to the mix. The energy in the room was unlike anything I have ever experienced in over ten years of working in the live music business.
We would like to thank everyone at Philips Arena, Ted Mankin & The Cellar Door Team, and all the other Production Companies for an unforgettable night. And of course... Thanks so very much to the Fans who made it all possible.
Peter Jackson
What's been going on...
In between checking in with John Keane at the studio and spending time at home, the band has had plenty to keep them busy. All sorts of side projects going on from Orlando, FL to Oxford, MS.
The first order of business was to support JB in his plight to raise funds and awareness for his favorite cause:
Hannah's Buddies , a non-profit organization concerning Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA). Trey Allen (the band's road manager) filled us in on all the hooplah surrounding this now epic event.
Hannah's Charity Golf Classic
On January 9th 2000 at the House of Blues Orlando marked the first annual golf tournament and benefit concert. The golf tournament got underway at 8 am at the Disney Lake Buena Vista Course. The course was in great condition and allowed little error. Each player had an impact on each teams success and failure, some more than others. The course's staff went beyond the call of duty to create a great
atmosphere. After a silent auction the music started with John Bell, Barbara-Cue and Bloodkin. The music was great but more importantly the fans were the heros of the evening. Thanks to those who lost golf balls and everyone involved. Duncan Elliot, Hannah's father had these comments...
"What a day from the first tee shot at 8 AM to the last song from Bloodkin; everyone had a good time in support of a worthy cause. Spinal Muscular Atrophy affects 25,000 children in the US today and is the number one cause of death in children under two. The research is moving fast and progress is being made every day. Thanks and love go out to everyone who participated! Together, we can make a positive difference in the lives of Hannah and others suffering from this disease".
JB had this to add: It's people joining together that makes things happen.
The Hannah's Buddies Charity event in Orlando was truly a hope-filled way to start the year 2000. Even in this modern age, there are still Dragons to be Slain, Hearts to be won, and plenty of old-fashioned good deeds to be done - and you folks were out there doing it all. On behalf of Hannah and all children born with Spinal Muscular Atrophy, I thank you all for your participation and kind thoughts. Love, JB
For more information on SMA, visit www.fightsma.com, and also keep your eye on the band's website for the next Hanna's Charity Golf Classic.
The next order of business for Trey was to get JoJo back to Oxford, MS for a Beanland reunion . Beanland (JoJo former group of southern road dogs) tries to get together at least once a year when George McConnell and Bill McRory, the band's founders, round all the old members up for a weekend of belting out rhythm and blues. When asked to describe the weekend, George recalls, "...a wonderful blur of good friends and good times. I think the best part for all of us, though, was getting to jam with the inimitable Duff Dorrough, guitarist with the Tangents, who were dubbed "the House Band of Mississippi" and a MAJOR influence on Beanland. I mean, that's who we wanted to be. JoJo always wanted to play like Catfish Mickey."
JoJo says, "Jamming with Duff at Proud Larry's was not only one of the highlights of the Beanland relapse weekend, but of any gig I can remember ever playing. The other thing was rehearsing out at Taylor, MS (which has the hippest mayor in America Ð Hey Jane Rule!) and then walking into Taylor Catfish and takin' a big Ôole fat whiff! Welcome back! And we were all glad Chico didn't sleep through it this year."
For more information on Beanland and any future relapses, you can check out their website: www.beanland.net
Next on the band's time off agenda, Barbara Cue, Bloodkin, and Brute. Richard Kherlopian (Widespread Merchandise) worked hard to put this one together so we'll let him explain this one.
First, we'd like to thank everyone who helped and attended this wonderful benefit to help two friends in need. Lee Laurence and David Blackmon.
Before we go any further, some of you may not know of two tragic occurrences that occurred last summer to warrant such a benefit. On the 4th of July here in Athens, David Blackmon, long-time fiddlin' friend of the band, broke his back while playing frisbee. He is recovering well but was unable to walk for some time. Later on in the summer, traveling in bad weather conditions in Massachusetts, the van and trailer that Day By The River (fellow Athens band) was traveling in was involved in an accident that took the life of the band's sound engineer Lee Laurence. Also the insurance on their tour van had lapsed and most of their gear was damaged. Without insurance the band was plunged deeply in debt. A week after the accident the band decided to break up after being together for 10 years.After booking the Georgia Theatre for a date in late January, Bloodkin and Barbara Cue (Todd Nance's side project) agreed to play. Not really knowing whom else was going to show up to play this benefit, Bill "Gomer" Jordan had spoken to Vic Chesnutt about performing. Vic had the time off from touring and agreed to attend. Before too long all the members of Widespread Panic planned on performing. John Keane also showed up with his pedal steel to complete the night. This had turned into a full-fledged Brute Show . The show was a great success! Special thanks to everyone at the Georgia Theater, as well as Brian Britingham, Zac Weil, Wayne Sawyer, Garrie Vereen, Peter Jackson, Trey Allen and every one else who made this a special night.
As you can see, Todd can't get enough of the road with Panic, so not only has Barbara Cue lended their talents to these great causes, but they are touring when they get a chance. You can see what they are up to on their website:
www.barbaracue.com.
Low and behold, there are Stained Souls among us. You have to love it when Col. Bruce and Tinsley Ellis get a chance to come out and jam with some of the boys. Peter Jackson stole the Col. for a few moments while he was in Athens to get a brief history of The Stained Souls:
As many of you may know by now, there is a musical powerhouse that comes together in the winter months called The Stained Souls. The Stained Souls are a continually morphing group of jamming musicians that are anchored by Col. Bruce Hampton and Bluesman Extraordinaire Tinsley Ellis. According to Bruce and Tinsley, The Stained Souls( AKA The Modest Mariners) have convened during the winter months every year since 1985. Sometimes it would just be Bruce and Tinsley and other times with such folks as Otiel Brubridge and longtime Bruce cohort Ricky Keller. To quote Tinsley, "it was just one big loose cannon."Other stories of "the early days" tell tales of a young college student named Dave Schools peeking around the corner of the old Uptown (when he was still the doorman there!) with a look of "terrified curiosity" on his face upon hearing the sound of the Souls in 1986. Then in 1998 the present form came into being. Todd, JOJO, Dave, Bruce and Tinsley came together at The Star Bar in Atlanta. This was a very small place that got packed to the gills with folks who raced from all over the Southeast to get a chance to see this group of musicians play in such a small setting. The night was considered a huge success thus planting the seed for more shows with the current line-up. 1999 saw a stellar Stained Souls exhibition at The Variety Playhouse also in Atlanta. JOJO delivered his usual style of aggressive keyboard tactics assaulting his organ with a fine-tuned plumber's friend. Even JB stopped by to pitch in on some of the action.So, this year (the 15th Anniversary!!) the Souls returned to Athens and the legendary Georgia Theater for a huge night of JAM. Those lucky enough to find a ticket heard a wealth of spontaneous rock and roll that included such classics as Stagger Lee, Stormy Monday, and Suzie Q. Thanks to everyone who made it to the shows and in winter months of the years to come, keep your ears open for news of yet another interplanetary installment of The Stained Souls.
Sunny will be conducting another series of Beat The Drum on June 12 at the GA Theater here in Athens. The drum extravaganza benefits the youth program "Drumming for Success" which is directed by it's founder, Dr. Arvin Scott. The band is donating a big item to this years Human Rights Festival Auction which also benefits Drumming for Success: a brand new conga donated by Latin Percussion and autographed by the band that comes with a lesson from Sunny and Todd. So, come on over to Athens for the auction and put your bid in. The band is also donating tickets for the Athens shows to The Athens Homeless Shelter and Child Abuse Council auctions. There will be more information and details on the band's website.

Widespread Panic / Brown Cat

issues.label: Vol. 6 #2
issues.title: Moon Times Vol. 6 #2
issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
Greetings From Brown Cat
It's crazy, business as usual around Brown Cat these days. Trying to get ready for summer hasn't allowed too much time to reflect, but Spring Tour was incredible and we want to thank everyone who came out to see the band. We think that the
three Athens shows that kicked off the tour will go down in the books as some of the best and the run of Myrtle Beach shows at House of Blues continues to be legendary.
Bernie Worell came out to see the band in New York City and sat in on "Red Hot Mamma", which he wrote. That was very exciting for the band.
Trey Allen, the band's road manager, spent a brief moment trying to pick a tour highlight and came back with a huge grin on his face and this graceful photo of
he and Todd on "The Hulk" at Universal Studios in Orlando. Wayne Sawyer spent a good part of spring tour helping everyone see all the highlights from tour on the band's website. His new On The Road section of the site became an instant success with his bi-weekly posts of set lists and his digitally enhanced photos taken backstage and from "Wayne's guitar world" on stage.
Nobody had to ask Peter Jackson, the band's production manager and Todd's drum tech, what his highlight of the tour was. The short version of the story is that at the show in Newark, DE, something happened that has only happened once in almost 15 years. Todd caught something nasty and was too sick to play (that's pretty sick). So, after an acoustic first half of the first set,
Peter sat in and played drums with the band for the rest of the show. We are a real team at Brown Cat and everyone always steps in wherever there is something to be done no matter what your job. Peter took this practice to a whole new level. And, to top it off, Rolling Stone was there and gave the band and Peter a glowing review. So, Peter will be leaving Brown Cat on the highest note possible. That's right, Peter is leaving us... Peter Jackson has been a loyal employee of Brown Cat, Inc. for over 4 years. He has been Todd's assistant as well as overseeing the stage production. Peter has recently decided to hang up his travelling spurs and has accepted a position with Technical Services Audio-Visual, an Athens based design and installation firm. Peter had this to say on his departure:
As I move into a new phase of my life (I have taken a new job that will keep me at home in Athens),
I wanted to take the chance to deliver a few parting words about life with Widespread Panic.
As the Band continues to grow and the number of new fans grow by leaps and bounds, I think we all need to consider what a wonderful environment the Band and the legion of older fans have built over the years. While the whole scene has gotten to be quite huge, there remains this underlying feeling that its still just like it used to be...just 6 regular guys playing good music to a bunch of regular folks that like to hear good music.
BUT... I have begun to notice recently that some of that family atmosphere is beginning to slip away. There is some truly disturbing behavior going on both in the Lot and in the Shows.
So, my message is this...To the fans, please take care of this beautiful thing that we call Widespread Panic. Because as the Band will tell you..its not just about the 6 of them, its about all of us.
So do what you can to respect yourself, your fellow fans, and the communities in which we are visitors. This will give us all the ability to continue to enjoy the music we love for years to come.
Thanks for all the memories.
I'll see you out in the crowd...
Peter Jackson
We are all very sad to see Peter go,
but must send him off with sincere
congratulations on a job very well
done and on his new career.
The folks in Widespread Merchandise have been stuck over at the new merchandise warehouse for weeks sending out more Another Joyous Occasion records than we ever imagined. The launching of
Widespread Records with Another Joyous Occasion has been quite a ride. Although with the Dirty Dozen on board, how could we go wrong? Of course we can't talk about the release of this record without mentioning all the release parties that happened. The coast to coast listening parties for Another Joyous Occasion were a huge success! The band sends out heartfelt thanks to all who made these celebrations possible. Panic fans, once again, have proven themselves to be the best! With the help of all of the venues that hosted parties, Another Joyous Occasion has been heard and celebrated from San Francisco to New York City to London. We don't need much more evidence of the power of this community. It's undeniable. Widespread Records has been an
adventure and now a success thanks to all of you. We hope everyone had a great time and is enjoying the record. We could not have done it with out you. THANK YOU!
There is more good news and congratulations to pass on. The band got home just in the nick of time for a new Houser arrival,
Eva Houser was born on Thursday, May 11th. Everybody is healthy, happy, and very proud.
JoJo and Christy Anderson married in Atlanta on Sunday, May 28th. It was a beautiful garden ceremony and JoJo was playing with half of his 8th grade band before it was all over. We're hoping someone had a video camera. Special thanks go out to all the fans on Spring Tour who contributed to Panic Fans For Food. Joshua Stack and Jennifer Jones (PFFF) report that 4,299 lbs of food were collected on tour.
FANTASTIC!! You guys keep up the good work.
Well, the count-down moves on to the end of Moontimes as we've known it for 6 years. If you missed the first issue of 2000 (Vol. 6 No. 1), as of 2001, Moontimes will only be available over the internet. Our end of the year issue will be the last issue of Moontimes in print. We'll go on about how sad yet happy that makes us in a later issue, but for now it is very important that you get us your email address. You can do that on the band's website
(www.widespreadpanic.com) by going to "Moontimes" on the site. Also, by signing up on email, we will be able to keep you informed, about once a month, on what is going on with the band and when special events take place. We have already started doing all sorts of fun things
on the site so we want to keep everyone up to date.
Well, that's it from the homefront. Summer tour is here and is jam packed with good times. Who wants to see Panic with Dylan and Lesh? Have a great summer and be safe.
Copyright © 2001
Widespread Panic / Brown Cat

issues.label: Vol. 6 #3
issues.title: Moon Times Vol. 6 #3
issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
With summer behind us, thanks to everyone who helped make summer tour the biggest yet. Before we fully embrace the fall season, we need to remind you of one very important thing. Send us your email address. Brian Sofer, the ultimate webmaster, has been busy building a brand new site that will launch in conjunction with the beginning of Fall Tour. We are very excited about new technology, new design, new photos, and in general a more interactive site for www.widespreadpanic.com. This will also include an all new online Moontimes. In case you haven't heard, as of 2001, Brown Cat will no longer publish Moontimes in paper form to be sent out via snail mail. It will only be available over the internet. If you haven't submitted your email address to the web site for updates, you will want to do that now so you don't miss anything.
Summer is past but not forgotten. Chris Rabold, the band's new production manager helped us reflect a little on this year's summertime Panic.
Intensity and inspiration were the themes of a tour that saw the band share the stage with both excellent players and classic performers alike. The tour began at Red Rocks Amphitheater where Chuck Leavell helped kick things off along with members of Groove Collective. The next night, regular guest Colin Butler of Big Ass Truck was on turntables and George McConnell of The Kudzu Kings sat in on Sunday. Phoenix, AZ was treated to an evening that featured the legendary
Taj Mahal . While the performance was moving to say the least, it should be noted that Taj also cooked a fine chicken dinner for everyone that night..........really! At The Warfield in San Francisco the band played host to Merl Saunders, Cecil "Peanut" Daniels, John Keane, and
Jorma Kaukonen of Jefferson Airplane and Hot Tuna. In NYC's Central Park, old friend Jerry Joseph hopped on stage. Danny and Eric from Bloodkin came around in Winston Salem as well as old friend, David Blackmon. The Colonel and Rev. Mosier stopped by in Birmingham. Even Stanley Jordan sat in for a spell in Keystone. In very fitting form,
The Dirty Dozen Brass Band were on hand to help close out a memorable summer tour.
Don't worry if you missed out on any of these spectacular moments of summer tour. Most of it was caught on film. You can read about Sweetwater Productions rendition of summer tour 2000 inside.
Aside from the many memorable events on stage, there were other great moments that deserve mention. Amazing artistic talent came out for the banner contest for Keystone, CO. Page Bryan & Hallie Mitchell and
Ron Orozco & Jill Ruska were the banner artist winners for their renditions of 'Summer Tour 2000'. Thanks to all who brought banners to Keystone for a truly special end of summer. Panic Fans For Food, along with all those who contributed to the cause, once again deserve a gold medal. $3000 in cash and 2500 pounds of food was collected in 11 different cities across the US this summer. We encourage PFFF and the fans to keep up this generous endeavor and leave something much appreciated behind in the communities we all visit on Panic journeys. Sunny, along with Todd and some of the crew, led pre-show drum clinics all over the country. Once home, Sunny was also able to join Dr. Arvin Scott for
Beat the Drum at the Georgia Theater in Athens, GA. Keep in touch with the web site to know when the next Drumming For Success benefit will be held. One problem we did encounter on summer tour will not be tolerated. There is a new practice of duplicating and selling tour passes in the lot. The sale of ANY pass at anytime, anywhere is illegal and will not be honored.
So, keep your money and avoid being asked to leave a show.
And last, but certainly not least...... Widespread Panic will be back in Atlanta, GA at Philips Arena for two nights, December 30 & 31, to celebrate, "The New Millenium 2001". Brown Cat is currently offering mail order tickets for the shows. You can find detailed (prices, deadlines, shipping) information on the web site or you can give Widespread Tickets a call at 706 543-7299 and we would be happy to fax you all of the instructions.
That's it for now from the home front. We are working like crazy to get the band back on the road for fall. Tour dates are inside so you can start making your Panic plans. Be safe and we'll see you on the road.
Peace.............Brown Cat
We first met the Hanson Brothers from Sweetwater Productions a few years back when they contacted Brown Cat to license The Takeout for their critically acclaimed independent film Scrapple. As film makers, their vision of Widespread Panic and the
surrounding community made them a perfect fit to go on tour with the band for the summer. They not only captured incredible moments both on and off stage, they have become good friends. You will not want to miss their story of Panic.
The Hanson Perspective
When
my brother Chris and I set out to make our first film, we showed our script to another producer. Upon reading the script and then asking us how much money we had to make it, he replied, "That's like trying to fit a size eight foot into a size three shoe." I was reminded of this sentiment when Mary asked me to write about my experience this summer documenting Widespread Panic's summer tour for a movie in 500 words. There are so many stories and feelings I would like to convey, but I want to leave it to the movie itself to express them. One story that sticks out that we regrettably did not capture on tape, is a story that Donnie Ruppert told me. Donnie is the lead truck driver for Widespread Panic. On the very first day of the tour at
Red Rocks , Donnie told me that when he started with Widespread Panic he couldn't for the life of him distinguish the band from the roadies. Usually, it's a snap. Look for the gregarious outfits, the giant egos, the ones throwing the temper tantrums, and there you have your rock stars. Since his job is to drive through the night and sleep until the show is over, he finally decided he had to attend a show so he could figure out who was in the band. To me, this sums up Widespread Panic in a nutshell. From Tony Ruffino, the promoter at Oak Mountain, to Julius, the backstage gate keeper at the Warfield, the words "kind, generous, humble, gracious, and genuine" were expressed to me over and over as I spoke to people all over the country about Widespread Panic. I believe this spirit of generosity is reflected in the band's performance. They are generous to each other as musicians and generous to us as fans. I witnessed 22 concerts this summer, and each time the boys entered the sacred space that is the stage, they kicked it down with all their collective heart and soul, searching for that place where the music plays the band. And this spirit of generosity permeates the entire organization. From top to bottom, Brown Cat is a model institution and nothing short of a minor miracle.I'd like to thank Sam and Buck for giving us a chance; Mary for believing in and nurturing the vision; Trey, Draper and Shaw for making it all happen; Chris, Garrie and the rest of the crew for putting up with us; and the band for the inspiration. It was a thrill and an honor to be a part of the road show this summer.
Over the summer,
my brother Geoff and I had the opportunity to travel with Widespread Panic and shoot them on mini digital video. We ended up shooting close to 300 hours of footage over 20 dates all over the country. Now we have the daunting task of editing our footage into the semblance of a movie. You can expect to see the finished version in the fall of 2001. I have no idea what will make it into the final video, and what will end up on the cutting room floor, but I do have an idea of what I experienced this summer. Here's a trailer of the movie currently playing in my head, but don't hold me accountable for any of it.
Opening titles...fade into Red Rocks show...Thought Sausage montage...to Oak Mountain...fans go nuts to Burning Down The House...Cut to Central Park, Jerry Joseph plays with Todd, Mikey, and Dave on the Alice in Wonderland sculpture...Cut to Harmony Park, Geneva, Minnesota...Dream Catcher...Primera Casa...Cut back to Central Park show, Go North...Col. Bruce talks with Geoff from hotel lobby in Sesquachanan...Dissolve to San Francisco, Jorma joins the band for rehearsal in the basement of the Warfield...Catch a baseball game at Pacific Bell Park, dissolve to Willie Mays sculpture outside ballpark...Back to 4th of July concert...Peanut, Merl, John Keane, Jorma...Cut to Vegas...JB and JoJo drink Blood Ale at the Star Trek Bar...Back to Phoenix...Taj cooks barbeque for the crew...She Caught The Katy into...Sound check Oak Mountain...Cut to crew breaking down stage at Merriweather...Setting back up in North Carolina...Danny& Eric (Bloodkin), and Dave play End Of The Show with JB in a barn...Dissolve to animation...Back to reality...Action Man, Action Figures...Sonny leads drum circle...Dissolve to Drums at Red Rocks with Colin Butler on turntable...Todd gets Down...show 31, we finally are dressed in black...Zoom into close up of fingers picking guitars...Fingers playing keyboards...Fingers slapping bass...percussion pull us back...low spark of imitation leather shoes...Dirty Dozen, Karl Denson & Stanley Jordan kiss the mountain air in Keystone...Cut back to red lights, blue lights, the note eater spins another concert...on to another town...Roadshow wraps another tour...roll credits.
I too would like to thank all the folks at Brown Cat, from the front office on through to the back stage crew, who made this summer such a treat.
Copyright © 2001
Widespread Panic / Brown Cat
