issues.label: Vol. 6 #4
issues.title: Moon Times Vol. 6 #4
issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
The end of 2000 has crept up on us like a cat on the prowl ready to pounce. The last pounce for Widespread Panic this millenium will be another two night stand at Philips Arena in Atlanta. That's right, 2001 is the official beginning of the new millenium. Through out all of the hoopla of New Years 2000 and the "millenium bug", Dave was incessant in reminding everyone that the real new millenium wasn't until new years 2001. Not that we need any more reason to celebrate, it's going to be the biggest party of the year no matter how you look at it. Pure Panic both nights with no openers. We look forward to celebrating with you.
Fall tour is in mid swing right about now and there are already a few must mention highlights. (Pre) Halloween in New Orleans is always worth mentioning. The stage was turned into a pirate ship the third night for the occasion and the Dirty Dozen Brass Band came on board for a rousing show. The band spent Halloween day in Austin, TX filming an episode of Austin City Limits that will be aired on December 30th on PBS. Check you local PBS station to see when and at what time it will be aired in your area. After a day of golf with JB, The Doors guitarist, Robbie Krieger, played with the band in Los Angeles at the Wiltern Theatre. You will find set lists and photos in 'On The Road' on the web site.
The all new widespreadpanic.com was launched with a bang at the beginning of Fall tour. As with many new things, we suffered from some unexpected growing pains immediately when the extremely high traffic shut down the server. We apologize for the inconvenience and have upgraded to be able to handle all of the traffic. There are hundreds of new pages to browse with videos, music, weekly updates, and just about anything you need to know about Widespread Panic.
The Disney Golf Course and House of Blues will play host once again next year to Hannah's Buddies Charity Classic on January 6. There will be a golf tournament during the day and music at House of Blues that night. JB and Kevn Kinney will be swapping songs to open the evening. More information will be posted soon on the 'Side Stage' of the web site. You can also find information on the 'Side Stage' about JoJo's recently recorded album to be released on Fat Possum Records. Word is that Luther and Cody Dickinson (North Mississippi Allstars), Paul 'Crumpy' Edwards (Bloodkin), George McConnell (Kudzu Kings), and Cary Hudson (Blue Mountain) got together in Mississippi and, "had a blast."
So, here it is, the last printed issue of Moontimes. As we look back on all of the archive Moontimes, it is a wonderful representation of where the band has been and how far they have come in the past six years. The first Moontimes came out in the Winter of 95 sending out thanks to Ben Tanen and Lee Silverman for creating the Spreadnet. The next issue, Volume 1 No. 2, announced the presence of the new cyberspace Widespread Panic homepage, widespreadpanic.com, and the next issue offered the very first mail order tickets for two Halloween shows here in Athens at the Classic Center Exhibition Hall. The first issue of Vol. 2 in 1996 gave a report from the 'Sit and Ski' tour. By the end of 96, we were in color and offering mail order for New Years at the Fox. Vol. 3 saw the release of 1997 Bombs and Butterflies, Todd's bbq expertise, Gomer's 'The Bottom Line', and pictures of the band with Billy Bob Thornton and Fabio. Vol. 4 told tale of the massive, world record setting album release party in Athens on April 18, 1998 for the band's first live release, Light Fuse Get Away. There was also the band's first European tour and a summer package including other acts such as Galactic, G-Love and Special Sauce, Gov't Mule, and Leftover Salmon. Vol. 5 began while the band was in the studio diligently working on 'Til The Medicine Takes in the winter of 1999. TTMT came out that summer while the band was on their first romp with The Dirty Dozen Brass Band. The last Vol, took on a new design and look to stir things up a bit since we knew then it would be the last volume. 2000 saw the inception of Widespread Records with it's first release, Another Joyous Occasion and the Hanson Brothers spent the summer on the road with the band filming a documentary that will be released in 2001. You can see the complete archives of Moontimes on the new website.
There are few things we won't miss about Moontimes in print. I know Ellie isn't going to cry because she doesn't have 3000 addresses to take off of the list one by one after each mailing. The band's booking agency isn't going to miss me calling them every minute to get all of the tour dates so Moontimes could go to print in time to be mailed before the tour. I will miss working with Keith Guest at Guest printing who always put up with me bringing in film and letting him know that I need it by yesterday. Tom and Sharon out at Athens Mailing Service have taken very good care of Moontimes and Brown Cat and will be missed. On the other side, we look forward to expanding Moontimes over the internet. You'll still get the same stories, pictures, and updates, but in a much more interactive form. Keep in touch with the web site to see when the first cyber Moontimes will be posted.
It's been yet another great year for all of us and we look forward to more of the same in 2001. We wish everyone a happy and safe holiday season and we'll see you next year.
Peace and love………………..Mary
Copyright © 2001
Widespread Panic / Brown Cat

issues.label: Vol. 7 #1
issues.title: Moon Times Vol. 7 #1
issues.attribution: Moon Times was the official Widespread Panic newsletter, produced by the WSP office / Brown Cat. Text preserved by the Internet Archive (web.archive.org) from widespreadpanic.com's own site.
issues.text:
Hello everybody!! Welcome to the newest incarnation of Moontimes, available online only at Widespreadpanic.com. In the past, we've had a hard time squeezing all of our articles, photos, and information on to just a few pages of print. It's a whole new ballgame now. By offering Moontimes via cyberspace we now have, well…..more space. You'll now have access to tons of photos, links to articles, site updates, digital video, artwork, tour dates, and whatever else we are able to throw your way. You should also take the opportunity to browse around the site and see what's new. The current events section of
Panic Plaza's News Stand is always a great place to start. You will want to check in at least once a week to see what our favorite webmaster, Brian Sofer, has picked out as the live weekly MP3 at the
Soundboard . We are just getting started with this new venture so sit back, take your time, and let us know what you think.
Once again the band has spent the majority of the winter months over at
John Keane's studio working on a new record.
We've left it up to our man
Billy Field to fill you in on what all that entails .
Billy has assisted John on every album since Bombs & Butterflies in 1996 and is an integral part of the road crew as well. For this latest endeavor, John has enlisted the service of engineer Doug Trantow. Doug not only has a talented ear, but a vast knowledge of fine wines as well, the latter of which did not go unnoticed. Our friends the
Hanson Brothers took some time away from editing live footage of the band, to come down to Athens to fill in that last important aspect of Widespread Panic; in the studio with John Keane. The Hanson's weren't the only ones with camera in hand.
Guitar tech Sam Holt was on board during basic tracking sessions and took most of the still photos that you see here. Merchandise manager, Richard Kherlopian,also spent some time behind the lens. Check out his video for an insider's look at Panic in the studio.
While the album itself makes it to tape, there are others working diligently on what you will see as the finished product. Among those people is artist Flournoy Holmes. You may not recognize the name, but you definitely know his work. Flournoy has served as art director for past records Space Wrangler, 'Til The Medicine Takes, and Another Joyous Occasion. The band is very lucky to be able to work with talents such as Flournoy's.
Read his bio and we think you'll agree.
A few of the band members are going to take some time to answer questions you may have about the studio in an interview that we will post on the web site on Friday March 16th. (Submit your questions here) and then we'll conduct an interview with as many of those questions as we can and post it on the site in an audio file complete with photos of the interview. We will send you a reminder when it goes up on the site.
This latest project will be released this summer with the help of
Sanctuary Records international staff. We are all very excited about this partnership and look forward to working together. In a press release related to the signing of the band to Sanctuary, JB said,
"When musicians combine their talents in ways that feed and magnify a creative environment; that's the magic of playing in a band. When you add to the mix a record label that recognizes its own unique abilities, as well as those of the artists, you get even more creative fuel - that's what Widespread Panic and Sanctuary have in each other - and this particular party is just getting started." Sanctuary would like to carry on the tradition the fans and the band started a few years ago with release/listening parties all over the US. So, just like you did last year,
fill out this questionnaire , and along with Sanctuary we are going to have as many parties as possible. That means in your town, at your favorite place, with all of your friends. This questionnaire will remain on the current events page of Panic Plaza's News Stand for awhile so have everyone you know in your town fill one out to make sure we know where you want to have your party. We'll organize it, send music, posters, and whatever else we can think of to make it a success.
The studio isn't the only thing that has been keeping the band busy while off of the road. JB hosted another successful charity event for
Hannah's Buddies in Orlando. JoJo went to Mississippi and did a record for Fat Possum Records called
The Smiling Assassin .
Slang , Dave's side project with partner
Layng Martine , will release their record The Bellweather Project on Terminus Records this spring while he
is busy on the road with Government Mule. You can find those tourdates on the
Gov't Mule site . Sunny and Dr. Arvin Scott got a chance to do another 'Beat The Drum' at the Georgia Theater in Athens in support of
Drumming for Success . Todd has gotten out and played a few shows with
Barbara Cue .
Folks lucky enough to get a ticket to the Mental Health Benefit at the 40Watt in Athens, saw a great show including a set with Barbara Que where JoJo and JB floated on and off stage. Then the audience was treated to a surprise
brute. set with Vic Chesnutt. Another fantastic show for another great cause.
As you can see,
the crew has been very busy getting everything ready to get the band on the road. The southeast is the recipient of a short but very sweet spring tour this year. Here is a
printable version (PDF file) of the tourdates only available through Moontimes. Tickets are already on sale for all of the shows and are going fast. You can get detailed tour and ticket information in
Tourdates . If you don't get a chance to get out and see the band this Spring, our man Steve "Loopy" Lopez will keep you up to date with photos and commentary in
On The Road .
Everyone at Brown Cat has stayed pretty busy this winter, especially Ellie. She has been away from Brown Cat, busy having a baby. Conrad Angus MacKnight was born on January 17 and weighed in at an unbelievable 10 pounds and almost 2 ounces. She'll be back in time for Red Rocks mail order, which Brown Cat will begin accepting orders for on Monday, March 19. The dates for Red Rocks 2001 are June 22nd, 23rd, and 24th.
Link to Red Rocks Mail Order information in Panic Plaza's Box Office for all instructions.
Thanks for stopping by. We hope you enjoy this new online version of Moontimes. Don't hesitate to let us know what you think, what we might be missing, or if you have any questions . It's hard to comprehend what a profound effect the internet has had on the way we live our lives. Brown Cat is no exception. It was only about 4 years ago that when we posted tour dates on the hotline (remember the hotline…..the answering machine with the dates on it), we would have to keep an eye on it all day and keep erasing the hundreds of calls so it would continue to pick up. We would even find ourselves making several visits to the office over the weekend to do the same. Today is a completely different story. About 3 weeks after this spring tour for 2001 was posted on the hotline, we erased only 11 calls. Meanwhile, the site is averaging 250,000 hits a day. Pass this address on to your friends. We want to make sure Moontimes is still reaching everyone it should.
Take care, be safe on the road and we'll see ya.
Peace…………..Brown Cat

## Billy
Thank you so much for this opportunity and occasion to unbelievably embarrass myself.
…Billy
The other day I was at the Keane Compound being tortured by Sunny to learn his new toy and its usage while John Keane is screaming something to the effect of "cue up reel 12". My mind started to snap, crackle, and pop due to a phone conversation that included 1 attractive office manager, 1st cyber Moontimes, writing an article, and myself. Oh what to do…what to do. So here goes, I will attempt to describe to you whatever the hell it is that I do and see in this fantasy land of Panic. Fair warning though, I sure the hell was not an English major in college. Speaking of which, I attended an infamous technical school, graduating 5th in my class, with numerous awards, and relocated to Athens, to start working with John Keane. No day is ever the same here (sounds like a tour I know). Today, everybody is here for basic tracks. The phone is ringing, the Loco's guy is at the door wanting cash, JB is in the hallway swinging an imaginary golf club, Sunny's knee deep in some kind of catalog, and Doug's raving about coffee. Miscellaneous things are happening in the front lounge, South Park is on the tube, Mikey wants new strings, Dave wants his fretless here, John wants more mics, stands, and cables in the back room wired to inputs 1 thru 4, its only 1:30, and where's Jojo?
Agghhhhh… Sheik!! I have had the pleasure of assisting John on sessions with the likes of Panic, Brute, Vic Chesnutt, R.E.M., 10,000 Maniacs, and the list goes on. This is the place where it all happens. Songs are created, guitars are brought out, Zack (the cat) is bummed out, and Dave is hangin' out. Usually, this is the spot where I start muttering on and on about all the things I see and hear going on around me in the studio. Unfortunately, not only do I have poor eyesight, but can't hear to well either.
After being bent, molded, and scolded into place and into the world of studio recordings by the Tempo Nazi. I was instructed to go out on the road. The proper channels were taken, an auction was running on Ebay to win the tech. support of 1 Billy Field. With numerous bids placed, and people haggling, dollar signs flying around, the high bidder turned out to be DSCHOOLS@widespreadpanic.com. I was sent out into the wild blue yonder to keep the guys maintained. I have yet to ask which category I fell under, but have since been given the task to care and maintain for 1 David Schools including, but not limited to, all necessary instruments and equipment, while taking on 8 digital tape decks, and a rehearsal room. Cities now come and go so often, as do crew members, I have many wonderful memories to retain. A certain one to recall is visiting the Glacier National Park with Todd, Sunny, Jojo, and Eric Draper. Another would be white water rafting with Spidey, and Eric while dealing with some local law enforcement in Idaho. Oh what a day.
…….Bill E. Field

## Hansons
I'm sitting in my office watching footage of the boys in John Keane's studio.
We had five cameras running on that day - January 25. We shot about 18
hours of footage on that day alone. With that kind of burn rate, it's easy to
see how we accumulated 500 hours of footage over the course of shooting the
movie. The 500 hours represents about 50 unique hours of content.
We go through the tapes and try to eliminate as much "fat" off the footage as
possible. Chris has done most of the initial editing, though he is working
in Denver now editing concert footage from Red Rocks and Oak Mountain so I'm
helping with a few of the sessions. Once we edit down our original masters
(one of the great advantages of digital video is you can make transfers
without losing a generation), we then "media log" the footage - assign a
numerical sequence to all the footage.
It is our hope that the movie will be between 75-100 minutes long. We've
been told we can fit 180 minutes onto a DVD and you can be sure we will use
every second available. Our projected release date is October 15, so please
let all your loved ones know that this movie (and maybe even a dvd player!)
is at the top of your Christmas 2001 wish list.
Before the release, we hope to screen the movie all over the country, much
like the album release parties that were held around the time of the AJO
release. If you think you might be interested in helping put together a
public screening in your town, please email me at
wpmovie@aol.com and we can
begin working on it.
We'll keep you updated through Moontimes on our progress and we plan to
launch a site for the movie on the internet in the next few months. A big
thanks to John Keane for letting us invade his studio, and to Peter Couhig
down here in Baton Rouge. His help has been instrumental in the production
of this movie. Back to the footage.
Geoff Hanson

issues.attribution: The Panicle was a fan-run Widespread Panic newsletter (Athens, GA, "from the Proving Ground"). This issue was transcribed by the "Nothing But Widespread Panic" fan blog (widespread-panic.blogspot.com). Credit to the blog and the original Panicle authors.
issues.text:
July 1992 THE PANICLE Newsletter
WSP Newsletter, the Panicle, in the July 1992 (Volume 1, Number 5) edition:
"Gomero Blanco" First recorded during the May 1987 sessions at John Keene's Studio in Athens for the initial release of "Space Wrangler". While this song is not played live, several shows at the Uptown Lounge featured similar sounding jams.
"Puppet Song" This was a very early version of "Pigeons" that was played in the summer of 1987. When JB added lyrics that August, the song obviously became "Pigeons".
"Eliza's Apartment" This sexy, melodic tune also debuted in the summer of 1987. At the time it was commonly heard as a lead into "Pigeons" and, to a lesser extent, "Conrad". ["Eliza's Apartment" appears on Ain't Life Grand. It is called "L.a." there.]
"The Take Out" Usually heard directly before "The Porch Song", this can be found on the "Space Wrangler" CD. Debuted in late 1987.
"The Machine" The hardest driving of all the Panic instramentals, this song is often heard as a lead into "Barstools". This favorite debuted in the fall of 1988. "The Machine" was the last song to be cut off the "Widespread Panic" CD, allowing the band to add the three minute jam that turned "Mercy" into a classic.
The following three songs, all written about the same time, are the ones that cause people the most confusion. They are all commonly mistaken for one another, and also referred to by totally different names. The original source for many of the different title's is Larry's Code. Larry, the drumming roadie, has his own titles for many of the songs, some of them caught on among the fans.
* "Disco" The first of these three debuted in January 1989. "Disco" is the funkiest of the Panic instramentals and has kind of a Mother's Finest groove to it. This tune returned to the play list on 5-19-92 in Memphis after an absence of about two years. "Disco" is very often mistaken for "B of D" and is also called "Storm Watch" (Larry's Code). Usually heard in the middle of "Driving Song".
* "A of D" This also debuted in early 1989 and was first called "Eat the Beet", a title Mike came up with after completing a Tim Robbins novel in which beets were the key ingredient in a perfume needed to save the world. "A of D" was the first of two instramentals recorded during the March 1989 Keene sessions that is played in the key of D -- thus the title "A of D". Larry's code for this one is "Morning Daydream".
* "B of D" The second of the two "D" instramentals also debuted early in 1989. This was often heard during "Driving Song" while "Disco" was off the playlist. ("A of D" could be heard there also, but less frequently). Larry's Code-- "Giving".
"Walkin'" Debuted as an instrumental at one of the acoustic shows done by JB and Mike in the spring of 1989. Lyrics were soon added and this went on to become the initial release on Panic's first major release. The instrumental jam between the second and third verses is sometimes called "Boogie in your Nose" by band members.
"The Earth Will Swallow You" The mellowest (yet probably the most intense) of the instramentals debuted on April 13, 1990, in Athens. It is extremely rare to hear this amazingly picturesque tune. Appropriately, Panic used this song to open the second set on June 7, 1990, in Dahlonega, GA, as 5000+ fans attempted to jam back into "The Cave", creating the illusion of the Earth swallowing the mass of people.
"Untitled Instrumental" Written in the summer of 1990 at the same time as "Fishwater" and "Dog Song", this song debuted July 25 at the Post Office in Augusta, Ga. It was played again two nights later at the Cotton Club in Atlanta but it was hardly ever played following that night. The song was used as a lead into "Travelin' Light" both nights. At the time, JB was working on lyrics for the tune, but that never materialized.
"Astronomy Domine": An obscure Pink Floyd song, this is the only instrumental cover by Panic. This rarity is a treat and is usually only heard during a truly raging set.
